beginner.explanation: 'Christ' is not Jesus' last name — it is a title from the Greek 'Christos,' meaning 'The Anointed One.' It is the Greek equivalent of the Hebrew word 'Messiah' — the promised Saviour of the world.

beginner.question: Peter is often considered the leader of the early church.
beginner.explanation: Jesus told Peter 'On this rock I will build my church.' After Jesus ascended, Peter became the main spokesperson for the apostles. He preached the first sermon at Pentecost and led the early church in Jerusalem.

beginner.question: Jesus said 'You are the salt of the earth.'
beginner.explanation: In the Sermon on the Mount, Jesus told His followers 'You are the salt of the earth.' Salt was used to preserve food and add flavour — Jesus meant that His followers should have a positive, preserving influence on the world.

beginner.question: Jesus described Himself as 'the bread of life.'
beginner.explanation: Jesus said 'I am the bread of life. Whoever comes to me will never go hungry, and whoever believes in me will never be thirsty.' He meant that He alone satisfies the deepest hunger of the human soul.

beginner.question: When Jesus raised Lazarus, He called out 'Lazarus, come out!'
beginner.explanation: Jesus stood before the opened tomb and cried out in a loud voice 'Lazarus, come out!' And the dead man came out, his hands and feet wrapped in strips of linen and a cloth around his face.

beginner.question: In the parable of the wise and foolish builders, both houses survived the storm.
beginner.explanation: Only the house built on rock survived. The house built on sand fell with a great crash when the rain, floods, and wind beat against it. Jesus used this to teach about the importance of obeying His words.

beginner.question: Stephen was the first Christian to be killed for his faith.
beginner.explanation: Stephen was a faithful believer who was arrested and brought before the Jewish council. After giving a bold speech about Jesus, the angry crowd dragged him out and stoned him to death, making him the first Christian martyr.

beginner.question: Jesus once fell asleep in a boat during a storm.
beginner.explanation: While crossing the Sea of Galilee, a furious storm arose but Jesus was sleeping peacefully in the stern of the boat. The terrified disciples woke Him, and He calmed the storm with just His words.

beginner.question: The New Testament was originally written in Latin.
beginner.explanation: The New Testament was originally written in Greek — specifically Koine (common) Greek, which was the widely spoken language across the Roman Empire in the first century. Latin was the official language of Rome, but Greek was the common tongue.

beginner.question: Jesus healed people on the Sabbath (the day of rest), which upset the religious leaders.
beginner.explanation: Jesus healed people on the Sabbath multiple times, which angered the Pharisees who believed healing counted as 'work' forbidden on the Sabbath. Jesus said 'The Sabbath was made for man, not man for the Sabbath.'

beginner.question: Jesus was born during the reign of King Herod.
beginner.explanation: Matthew's Gospel tells us 'After Jesus was born in Bethlehem in Judea, during the time of King Herod...' Herod the Great ruled Judea under Roman authority at the time of Jesus' birth.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Prodigal Son, the younger son ended up feeding pigs after he spent all his money.
beginner.explanation: After wasting all his inheritance on wild living, the son was so desperate he took a job feeding pigs — one of the most degrading jobs for a Jewish person. He was so hungry he longed to eat the pig food.

beginner.question: The devil tempted Jesus only once in the desert.
beginner.explanation: The devil tempted Jesus three times, not once: first to turn stones into bread, then to throw Himself off the Temple, and finally to worship the devil in exchange for all the kingdoms of the world. Jesus refused all three.

beginner.question: After His resurrection, Jesus told Peter to 'Feed my sheep.'
beginner.explanation: Jesus asked Peter three times 'Do you love me?' and each time Peter said yes, Jesus said 'Feed my lambs' or 'Feed my sheep.' This reinstated Peter after his three denials and gave him the mission to care for Jesus' followers.

beginner.question: Jesus said 'Where two or three gather in my name, there am I with them.'
beginner.explanation: Jesus promised that whenever even a small group of believers comes together in His name, He is spiritually present with them. This encourages Christians to gather together in faith.

beginner.question: Luke wrote both the Gospel of Luke and the book of Acts.
beginner.explanation: Luke, who was a doctor and a companion of Paul, is credited with writing both books. He addressed both to a man named Theophilus. Together they make up about a quarter of the New Testament.

beginner.question: Paul and Silas sang hymns in prison, and an earthquake opened the doors.
beginner.explanation: Despite being beaten and chained, Paul and Silas prayed and sang hymns at midnight. A violent earthquake shook the prison, the doors flew open, and everyone's chains came loose. The jailer and his whole family became believers.

beginner.question: Jesus said 'I am the resurrection and the life.'
beginner.explanation: Jesus said this to Martha just before raising her brother Lazarus from the dead: 'I am the resurrection and the life. The one who believes in me will live, even though they die.'

beginner.question: The apostle John wrote the book of Revelation while on the island of Patmos.
beginner.explanation: John was exiled to the small island of Patmos because of his faith in Jesus. While there, he received visions from God about the end times, which he recorded as the book of Revelation.

beginner.question: Jesus said 'Ask, and it will be given to you; seek, and you will find.'
beginner.explanation: In the Sermon on the Mount, Jesus encouraged His followers to pray confidently: 'Ask and it will be given to you; seek and you will find; knock and the door will be opened to you.'

beginner.question: 1 Corinthians 13 is known as the 'love chapter' of the Bible.
beginner.explanation: Written by Paul, 1 Corinthians 13 describes the nature of true love: 'Love is patient, love is kind. It does not envy, it does not boast.' It is one of the most popular readings at weddings.

beginner.question: Jesus taught that the road to life is wide and easy.
beginner.explanation: Jesus taught the opposite: 'Wide is the gate and broad is the road that leads to destruction. But small is the gate and narrow the road that leads to life, and only a few find it.'

beginner.question: Before healing the paralysed man lowered through the roof, Jesus first said 'Your sins are forgiven.'
beginner.explanation: Jesus said 'Son, your sins are forgiven' before telling him to get up and walk. This shocked the religious leaders, but Jesus healed the man physically to prove He had authority to forgive sins too.

beginner.question: Jesus called Peter 'the rock' and said He would build His church on this rock.
beginner.explanation: Jesus said 'You are Peter (which means rock), and on this rock I will build my church, and the gates of death will not overcome it.' This gave Peter a central role in the founding of the Christian church.

beginner.question: John the Baptist called Jesus 'the Lamb of God.'
beginner.explanation: When John the Baptist saw Jesus coming toward him at the Jordan River, he said 'Behold, the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world!' This title pointed to Jesus as the ultimate sacrifice.

beginner.question: About 3,000 people were baptised on the day of Pentecost.
beginner.explanation: After Peter's powerful sermon on the day of Pentecost, about 3,000 people believed and were baptised. This was the explosive beginning of the Christian church.

beginner.question: Jesus said to love not only your friends, but also your enemies.
beginner.explanation: Jesus taught 'Love your enemies and pray for those who persecute you.' This radical teaching called people to love even those who do them harm — going far beyond what was expected.

beginner.question: The name 'Jesus' means 'The Lord saves.'
beginner.explanation: The name 'Jesus' comes from the Hebrew 'Yeshua,' meaning 'The Lord saves' or 'God is salvation.' The angel told Joseph to name Him Jesus 'because he will save his people from their sins.'

beginner.question: Saul was blinded for three days after his encounter with Jesus on the road to Damascus.
beginner.explanation: After the blinding light from heaven, Saul could not see for three days. He did not eat or drink anything. Then God sent Ananias to lay hands on him, and something like scales fell from Saul's eyes.

beginner.question: In the story of the Good Samaritan, a priest was the first person to stop and help the wounded man.
beginner.explanation: The priest did NOT stop to help — he saw the wounded man and walked past on the other side of the road. A Levite also passed by. It was the Samaritan — the least expected person — who stopped to help.

beginner.question: The Prodigal Son's father gave him a robe, a ring, and sandals when he returned home.
beginner.explanation: The overjoyed father called his servants to bring the best robe, put a ring on his son's finger, and sandals on his feet. He also ordered a feast to celebrate his son's return.

beginner.question: Jesus had at least four brothers.
beginner.explanation: The Gospels name four brothers of Jesus: James, Joseph (Joses), Simon, and Judas (not Judas Iscariot). He also had sisters. These were younger siblings born to Mary and Joseph.

beginner.question: Jesus prayed so intensely in Gethsemane that His sweat was like drops of blood.
beginner.explanation: In the Garden of Gethsemane, Jesus was in such anguish as He prayed about His coming suffering that His sweat was like drops of blood falling to the ground. An angel from heaven appeared to strengthen Him.

beginner.question: Paul was one of the original 12 apostles who walked with Jesus during His ministry.
beginner.explanation: Paul was NOT one of the original 12. He became a follower of Jesus later, after the resurrection, through a dramatic encounter on the road to Damascus. Before that, he had been persecuting Christians.

beginner.question: Jesus said 'I am the way, the truth, and the life.'
beginner.explanation: Jesus made this declaration to His disciple Thomas: 'I am the way and the truth and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me.' It is one of the most well-known verses in the Bible.

beginner.question: The book of Acts tells the story of how the early church grew and spread.
beginner.explanation: Acts describes how Christianity spread from a small group of believers in Jerusalem to communities throughout the Roman Empire, through the work of the apostles — especially Peter and Paul.

beginner.question: Bartimaeus was a blind beggar who cried out to Jesus for healing.
beginner.explanation: Bartimaeus sat by the roadside begging near Jericho. When he heard Jesus was passing, he shouted 'Jesus, Son of David, have mercy on me!' Despite people telling him to be quiet, he kept calling out, and Jesus healed him.

beginner.question: Matthias was chosen to replace Judas as the 12th apostle.
beginner.explanation: After Judas' death, the remaining apostles chose his replacement by casting lots. Matthias was selected, restoring the number of apostles to 12 before the day of Pentecost.

beginner.question: In the parable of the sower, all the seeds grew into healthy plants.
beginner.explanation: Only the seeds that fell on good soil grew and produced a crop. Seeds on the path were eaten by birds, seeds on rocky ground withered, and seeds among thorns were choked. Only good soil produced fruit.

beginner.question: Jesus told a sleeping storm to 'Be still' and it immediately obeyed.
beginner.explanation: It was Jesus who was sleeping during the storm, not the storm that was sleeping. When the disciples woke Him, He stood up and told the wind and waves 'Quiet! Be still!' and the storm stopped immediately.

beginner.question: Jesus taught 'Love your enemies and pray for those who persecute you.'
beginner.explanation: This radical teaching from the Sermon on the Mount went far beyond what was expected. Jesus called His followers not just to tolerate their enemies, but to actively love them and pray for them.

beginner.question: The last words Jesus spoke before ascending to heaven were about receiving the Holy Spirit.
beginner.explanation: Jesus said 'You will receive power when the Holy Spirit comes on you; and you will be my witnesses in Jerusalem, and in all Judea and Samaria, and to the ends of the earth.' Then He was taken up to heaven.

beginner.question: Jesus came to earth to save the world, not to condemn it.
beginner.explanation: Jesus said 'For God did not send his Son into the world to condemn the world, but to save the world through him.' This is one of the most hopeful statements in the entire Bible.

beginner.question: According to the very first book of the Bible, who created the heavens and the earth?
beginner.explanation: The Bible opens with the words 'In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth.' This is the foundational statement of the entire Bible, establishing God as the Creator of everything.

beginner.question: In the book of Genesis, how many days did God take to create the world before resting?
beginner.explanation: God created the world in six days and rested on the seventh day. Each day of creation brought something new — light, sky, land, stars, animals, and finally humans.

beginner.question: After spending six days creating the world, what did God do on the seventh day?
beginner.explanation: After finishing His creation in six days, God rested on the seventh day and blessed it. This is the origin of the Sabbath — a day of rest — which became an important part of Jewish tradition.

beginner.question: On the very first day of creation, what was the first thing God said to bring into existence?
beginner.explanation: God's first spoken words in the Bible are 'Let there be light,' and light appeared. This happened on the first day of creation, separating light from darkness and creating day and night.

beginner.question: According to Genesis, on which day of creation did God make the sun, the moon, and the stars?
beginner.explanation: On the fourth day, God created the sun to rule the day, the moon to rule the night, and the stars. These were placed in the sky to separate day from night and to mark seasons and days.

beginner.question: In the creation story, God made humans in a special way. The Bible says God created mankind in whose image?
beginner.explanation: Genesis states that God created mankind in His own image — both male and female. This means humans have a unique dignity and value among all of God's creation.

beginner.question: When God looked at everything He had made during creation, what did He say about it?
beginner.explanation: After completing creation, God looked at everything He had made and declared it 'very good.' This phrase emphasises that the original creation was perfect and beautiful in God's eyes.

beginner.question: According to Genesis, what is the name of the very first man God created?
beginner.explanation: Adam was the first human being, created by God from the dust of the ground. God breathed life into him and placed him in the Garden of Eden to care for it.

beginner.question: Who was the very first woman in the Bible, created by God as a companion for Adam?
beginner.explanation: Eve was the first woman, created by God from one of Adam's ribs. God saw that it was not good for Adam to be alone, so He made Eve as his partner and companion.

beginner.question: What was the name of the beautiful garden where God placed Adam and Eve to live?
beginner.explanation: God planted the Garden of Eden and placed Adam there to work it and take care of it. It was a perfect paradise with every kind of tree that was pleasant to look at and good for food.

beginner.question: In the Garden of Eden, God told Adam and Eve they could eat from any tree except one. What was the forbidden tree called?
options: The Tree of Knowledge of Good and Evil
beginner.explanation: God allowed Adam and Eve to eat from any tree in the garden except the Tree of Knowledge of Good and Evil. He warned them that eating from it would bring death.

beginner.question: Which animal spoke to Eve and tempted her to eat the forbidden fruit in the Garden of Eden?
beginner.explanation: The serpent was described as the most cunning of all the animals God had made. It tempted Eve by questioning God's command and telling her she would not die if she ate the forbidden fruit.

beginner.question: After Adam and Eve disobeyed God by eating the forbidden fruit, what did God do?
options: He created a new garden for them
He sent them out of the Garden of Eden
beginner.explanation: Because Adam and Eve disobeyed God's command, He banished them from the Garden of Eden. God placed angels with a flaming sword to guard the entrance so they could not return to the Tree of Life.

beginner.question: According to Genesis, God formed Adam from what material?
beginner.explanation: God formed Adam from the dust of the ground and then breathed the breath of life into his nostrils. This is how Adam became a living being, according to Genesis.

beginner.question: According to Genesis, God created Eve from what part of Adam's body?
beginner.explanation: God caused Adam to fall into a deep sleep, then took one of his ribs and formed Eve from it. When Adam saw her, he recognised her as bone of his bones and flesh of his flesh.

beginner.question: Adam and Eve had two sons. The younger son was a shepherd and the older son was a farmer. The older brother killed the younger one out of jealousy. Who was the older brother who committed this crime?
beginner.explanation: Cain was the older brother who killed Abel. Both brought offerings to God, but God favoured Abel's offering. Cain became jealous and angry, and murdered his brother in a field.

beginner.question: After Cain killed his brother Abel, God confronted him. What famous question did God ask Cain?
options: Why did you eat the fruit?
beginner.explanation: God asked Cain, 'Where is your brother Abel?' Cain replied with his own famous response: 'Am I my brother's keeper?' God already knew what had happened but gave Cain a chance to confess.

beginner.question: When the world became very wicked, God decided to send a great flood. He chose one righteous man to build a large boat to save his family and animals. Who was this man?
beginner.explanation: Noah was the one righteous man God found in a world full of wickedness. God told Noah to build an ark and gave him specific instructions for its size and construction to survive the coming flood.

beginner.question: God told Noah to bring animals onto the ark before the great flood. How many of each kind of animal did Noah bring?
beginner.explanation: God instructed Noah to bring two of every kind of animal — one male and one female — onto the ark so that they could survive the flood and repopulate the earth afterward.

beginner.question: According to Genesis, how many days and nights did it rain during Noah's great flood?
beginner.explanation: It rained for 40 days and 40 nights during the great flood. The number 40 appears many times in the Bible and often represents a period of testing or judgement.

beginner.question: After the flood waters began to recede, Noah sent out a bird to see if the water had gone down. What kind of bird brought back an olive branch, showing dry land was near?
beginner.explanation: Noah first sent out a raven, then a dove. The dove came back with a freshly plucked olive leaf in its beak, which told Noah that the floodwaters were receding and trees were emerging from the water.

beginner.question: After the great flood, God made a promise to Noah that He would never flood the entire earth again. What sign did God place in the sky as a symbol of this promise?
beginner.explanation: God set a rainbow in the sky as a sign of His covenant with Noah and all living creatures. Whenever a rainbow appears, it serves as a reminder of God's promise never to destroy the earth with a flood again.

beginner.question: Noah had three sons who went on the ark with him. Which of these was one of Noah's sons?
beginner.explanation: Noah's three sons were Shem, Ham, and Japheth. All three sons and their wives were on the ark with Noah and his wife, making eight people total who survived the flood.

beginner.question: After the flood, people decided to build a tall tower to reach heaven. God confused their languages to stop them. What is this tower called?
beginner.explanation: The people built the Tower of Babel because they wanted to make a name for themselves and reach the heavens. God confused their language so they could no longer understand each other, and they scattered across the earth.

beginner.question: At the Tower of Babel, what did God do to stop the people from building the tower to heaven?
beginner.explanation: God confused the languages of the builders so they could no longer understand one another. Unable to communicate, they abandoned the project and scattered across the earth, forming different nations and language groups.

beginner.question: God called a man to leave his homeland and go to a new land, promising to make him the father of a great nation. Before his name was changed, what was Abraham originally called?
beginner.explanation: Abraham was originally called Abram, meaning 'exalted father.' God later changed his name to Abraham, meaning 'father of many nations,' as part of His covenant promise.

beginner.question: God promised Abraham that he would become the father of a great nation. What was the name of Abraham's wife?
beginner.explanation: Sarah was Abraham's wife. She was originally called Sarai, and God changed her name to Sarah. Despite being very old, God promised that she would have a son, which she did — Isaac.

beginner.question: God tested Abraham's faith by asking him to sacrifice his only son on a mountain. What was the name of this son?
beginner.explanation: God asked Abraham to sacrifice his beloved son Isaac as a test of faith. Abraham obeyed, but at the last moment, God sent an angel to stop him and provided a ram to sacrifice instead.

beginner.question: When God described how many descendants Abraham would have, He compared their number to what?
options: Grains of sand and stars in the sky
beginner.explanation: God told Abraham his descendants would be as numerous as the stars in the sky and the grains of sand on the seashore. This was a remarkable promise since Abraham and Sarah were very old and had no children at the time.

beginner.question: When three visitors told the elderly Sarah that she would have a baby, what was her reaction?
beginner.explanation: Sarah laughed to herself when she overheard the promise that she would have a son, because she was about 90 years old. In fact, the name Isaac means 'he laughs,' commemorating this moment.

beginner.question: God destroyed two wicked cities with fire and brimstone from heaven. Abraham's nephew Lot escaped, but his wife looked back and was turned into a pillar of what?
beginner.explanation: When God destroyed Sodom and Gomorrah, He allowed Lot's family to escape but warned them not to look back. Lot's wife disobeyed and looked back at the burning cities, and she was turned into a pillar of salt.

beginner.question: What were the names of the two wicked cities that God destroyed with fire from heaven in the time of Abraham?
beginner.explanation: Sodom and Gomorrah were two cities so wicked that God decided to destroy them completely. Abraham pleaded with God to spare them if even ten righteous people could be found, but there were not enough.

beginner.question: Abraham and Sarah had a son in their old age, as God had promised. What did they name this miracle baby?
beginner.explanation: Abraham and Sarah named their son Isaac, which means 'he laughs.' Sarah had laughed when told she would have a baby at her old age, and now she laughed with joy at his birth.

beginner.question: Isaac grew up and married a woman that Abraham's servant found for him. What was Isaac's wife's name?
beginner.explanation: Abraham sent his servant back to his relatives to find a wife for Isaac. The servant met Rebekah at a well, where she showed great kindness by offering water to him and his camels. She agreed to marry Isaac.

beginner.question: Isaac and Rebekah had twin sons who were very different from each other. One was hairy and loved hunting, while the other was quiet and stayed at home. Who were these twins?
beginner.explanation: Jacob and Esau were Isaac's twin sons. Esau was the firstborn — red and hairy — and became a skilled hunter. Jacob was quieter and preferred staying among the tents. Their rivalry is a major story in Genesis.

beginner.question: Esau, the firstborn twin son of Isaac, came home very hungry one day. He gave up his birthright (his special inheritance rights as the eldest) in exchange for what?
beginner.explanation: Esau came in from the field exhausted and starving. Jacob was cooking lentil stew, and Esau traded his birthright — his special rights as the firstborn — for a single bowl of food, showing how little he valued it.

beginner.question: When Isaac was old and blind, one of his sons disguised himself to trick his father into giving him the blessing meant for the firstborn. Who tricked Isaac?
beginner.explanation: With help from his mother Rebekah, Jacob dressed in Esau's clothes and put goatskins on his arms to feel hairy like Esau. The blind Isaac was fooled and gave Jacob the blessing meant for Esau.

beginner.question: Jacob had a dream while sleeping with his head on a stone. In the dream, he saw a structure reaching from earth to heaven with angels going up and down on it. What did he see?
beginner.explanation: Jacob dreamed of a stairway (sometimes called a ladder) reaching to heaven, with angels ascending and descending on it. At the top was God, who renewed the covenant promise He had made to Abraham and Isaac.

beginner.question: After wrestling with a mysterious figure all night long, Jacob's name was changed. What new name was he given?
beginner.explanation: Jacob wrestled with a man (believed to be God or an angel) until daybreak. The figure changed Jacob's name to Israel, meaning 'he struggles with God.' The twelve tribes of Israel are named after Jacob's sons.

beginner.question: Jacob had twelve sons who became the founders of the twelve tribes of Israel. Jacob loved one son more than the others and gave him a special gift. What was this gift?
beginner.explanation: Jacob gave his favourite son Joseph a beautiful coat of many colours (or a richly ornamented robe). This favouritism made Joseph's older brothers extremely jealous and set the stage for the dramatic story that followed.

beginner.question: Joseph, the son with the coat of many colours, had a special ability that impressed even the Pharaoh of Egypt. What could Joseph do?
beginner.explanation: God gave Joseph the ability to interpret dreams. He interpreted the dreams of fellow prisoners in Egypt and later Pharaoh's dreams about seven years of plenty followed by seven years of famine, which saved Egypt.

beginner.question: Joseph's brothers were jealous of him because their father favoured him. What did Joseph's brothers do to him?
options: They gave him a new coat
beginner.explanation: Joseph's brothers threw him into a pit and then sold him to travelling merchants for twenty pieces of silver. The merchants took Joseph to Egypt, where he was sold as a slave. His brothers told their father he had been killed by a wild animal.

beginner.question: After being sold into slavery and later imprisoned in Egypt, Joseph rose to a very high position. What role did Pharaoh give Joseph?
beginner.explanation: After Joseph correctly interpreted Pharaoh's dreams about seven years of plenty and seven years of famine, Pharaoh was so impressed that he made Joseph second-in-command over all of Egypt, putting him in charge of preparing for the famine.

beginner.question: Pharaoh had a troubling dream that no one could explain. In the dream, seven thin cows swallowed seven fat cows. What did Joseph say this dream meant?
options: Seven years of war followed by peace
Seven years of plenty followed by seven years of famine
beginner.explanation: Joseph explained that the seven fat cows represented seven years of abundant harvests, and the seven thin cows meant seven years of severe famine. He advised Pharaoh to store grain during the good years to survive the bad ones.

beginner.question: During the great famine, Joseph's brothers came to Egypt to buy food, not knowing their brother was in charge. Which country did Joseph's family originally come from?
beginner.explanation: Joseph's family — Jacob and his twelve sons — lived in the land of Canaan. When the famine struck, the brothers travelled to Egypt to buy grain, where they unknowingly bowed before their long-lost brother Joseph.

beginner.question: A Hebrew baby was placed in a waterproof basket and hidden among the reeds of a river to save him from being killed. He was found and raised by an Egyptian princess. Who was this baby?
beginner.explanation: Baby Moses was placed in a basket on the Nile River by his mother to save him from Pharaoh's order to kill all Hebrew baby boys. Pharaoh's daughter found him, felt sorry for him, and raised him as her own son in the royal palace.

beginner.question: Baby Moses was hidden in a basket on which famous river in Egypt?
beginner.explanation: Moses' mother placed him in a waterproof basket among the reeds of the Nile River, the great river of Egypt. His sister Miriam watched from a distance to see what would happen to him.

beginner.question: After Moses grew up and fled Egypt, he was working as a shepherd when God spoke to him from a remarkable sight. What did Moses see?
options: A burning bush that was not consumed
beginner.explanation: Moses saw a bush that was on fire but was not burning up. When he went closer to investigate, God spoke to him from the bush, calling Moses to go back to Egypt and lead the Israelites to freedom.

beginner.question: When God spoke to Moses from the burning bush, He gave Moses a mission. What did God tell Moses to do?
options: Lead the Israelites out of slavery in Egypt
beginner.explanation: God told Moses to go to Pharaoh and demand that he let the Israelites go free from slavery. Moses was reluctant at first, but God promised to be with him and gave him miraculous signs to prove his authority.

beginner.question: Moses had a brother who helped him speak to Pharaoh because Moses felt he was not a good speaker. What was this brother's name?
beginner.explanation: Aaron was Moses' older brother. When Moses told God he was not eloquent enough to speak to Pharaoh, God appointed Aaron to be Moses' spokesman. Together, they confronted Pharaoh and demanded freedom for the Israelites.

beginner.question: When Pharaoh refused to let the Israelites go, God sent a series of terrible punishments on Egypt. How many plagues did God send?
beginner.explanation: God sent ten plagues on Egypt to convince Pharaoh to release the Israelites. These ranged from turning water to blood and sending frogs, to darkness covering the land and finally the death of every firstborn son.

beginner.question: The very first plague God sent on Egypt through Moses was dramatic — He turned all the water in the Nile River into what?
beginner.explanation: In the first plague, Moses struck the Nile with his staff and all the water turned to blood. The fish died, the river stank, and the Egyptians could not drink from it. Yet Pharaoh still refused to let the Israelites go.

beginner.question: In the second plague of Egypt, God sent a massive swarm of a particular creature that covered the entire land — even entering houses and beds. What creature was it?
beginner.explanation: God sent frogs everywhere in Egypt — they came up from the rivers and were in the houses, bedrooms, ovens, and kneading bowls. Pharaoh begged Moses to remove them but still would not let the people go.

beginner.question: The tenth and final plague of Egypt was the most devastating. What happened in this last plague?
options: The sun disappeared for a week
Every firstborn son in Egypt died
beginner.explanation: In the final plague, the angel of death passed through Egypt and every firstborn son died — from Pharaoh's own son to the firstborn of prisoners. Only the Israelites were spared because they had marked their doors with lamb's blood.

beginner.question: On the night of the final plague, God told the Israelites to mark their doorposts with something so the angel of death would pass over their homes. What did they use?
beginner.explanation: Each Israelite family was to sacrifice a lamb and put its blood on the top and sides of their doorframe. When the angel of death saw the blood, he 'passed over' that house. This event is the origin of the Jewish festival of Passover.

beginner.question: After the Israelites finally left Egypt, Pharaoh changed his mind and sent his army after them. The Israelites were trapped with a body of water in front of them. Which sea did God part to let them cross?
beginner.explanation: God parted the Red Sea so the Israelites could walk across on dry ground. When the Egyptian army tried to follow, the waters came crashing back and the entire army was destroyed.

beginner.question: Who stretched out his hand over the Red Sea, and God parted the waters so the Israelites could cross on dry ground?
beginner.explanation: God told Moses to stretch his hand out over the sea. Moses obeyed, and God sent a strong east wind that divided the waters, creating dry ground for the Israelites to walk through safely.

beginner.question: After crossing the Red Sea, the Israelites wandered in the desert and were hungry. God sent a special food from heaven each morning for them to eat. What was it called?
beginner.explanation: God provided manna — a white, flaky substance that appeared on the ground each morning like dew. The Israelites collected it daily and used it to make bread. It tasted like wafers made with honey.

beginner.question: When the Israelites were thirsty in the desert, God told Moses to strike a rock with his staff. What came out of the rock?
beginner.explanation: When the Israelites complained about having no water, God instructed Moses to strike a rock with his staff. Water gushed out of the rock, providing enough for all the people and their livestock to drink.

beginner.question: How many years did the Israelites wander in the desert wilderness before entering the Promised Land?
beginner.explanation: The Israelites wandered in the desert for 40 years as a consequence of their lack of faith. When spies reported that Canaan was too difficult to conquer, the people refused to trust God, so that generation was not allowed to enter.

beginner.question: God gave Moses a set of important laws on a mountain. On which mountain did Moses receive the Ten Commandments?
beginner.explanation: Moses climbed Mount Sinai, where God gave him the Ten Commandments. The mountain was covered in smoke and fire, with thunder and lightning, and the people trembled at the base.

beginner.question: How many commandments did God give to Moses on Mount Sinai?
beginner.explanation: God gave Moses Ten Commandments, which include instructions like worshipping only God, not making idols, honouring parents, and not stealing, lying, or murdering. These became the foundation of Israel's laws.

beginner.question: The Ten Commandments were written on what material?
beginner.explanation: God wrote the Ten Commandments on two tablets of stone. When Moses came down from the mountain and saw the people worshipping a golden calf, he was so angry he smashed the tablets. God later made a new set.

beginner.question: The very first of the Ten Commandments tells the people they shall have no other gods before whom?
beginner.explanation: The first commandment is 'You shall have no other gods before me.' God declared that He alone is the true God who brought the Israelites out of slavery in Egypt, and they must worship only Him.

beginner.question: While Moses was on Mount Sinai receiving the Ten Commandments, the impatient Israelites below made an idol to worship. What was this idol shaped like?
beginner.explanation: While Moses was away on the mountain for forty days, the people pressured Aaron to make them a god they could see. Aaron collected their gold jewellery and fashioned it into a golden calf, which they worshipped.

beginner.question: After Moses died, God chose a new leader to take the Israelites into the Promised Land. Who was this new leader?
beginner.explanation: Joshua had been Moses' faithful assistant for many years. God chose him to succeed Moses and lead the Israelites across the Jordan River into the Promised Land of Canaan.

beginner.question: Under Joshua's leadership, the Israelites conquered a city whose walls famously fell down. What was the name of this city?
beginner.explanation: The fortified city of Jericho had massive walls, but God told Joshua the walls would fall. The Israelites marched around the city for seven days, and on the seventh day, after blowing trumpets and shouting, the walls collapsed.

beginner.question: God gave Joshua unusual instructions for conquering the city of Jericho. What did the Israelites have to do to make the walls fall down?
options: March around the city and blow trumpets
beginner.explanation: God told Joshua to have the Israelites march around Jericho once a day for six days, with priests blowing trumpets. On the seventh day, they marched around seven times, gave a great shout, and the walls collapsed flat.

beginner.question: In the time of the Judges, there was a man with extraordinary strength given by God. His long hair was the sign of his special vow to God. Who was this strongman?
beginner.explanation: Samson was a judge of Israel with superhuman strength given by God. He was a Nazirite — someone set apart for God from birth — and his uncut hair was the symbol of his vow and the source of his God-given power.

beginner.question: The strong man Samson was tricked into revealing the secret of his strength. A woman named Delilah discovered it was his hair and had it cut while he slept. What happened when Samson's hair was cut?
beginner.explanation: When Samson's hair was cut, his strength left him because he had broken his Nazirite vow to God. The Philistines captured him, blinded him, and put him in prison. Eventually his hair grew back and he regained his strength for one final act.

beginner.question: Samson's enemies, the Philistines, wanted to know the secret of his great strength. Who was the woman they sent to discover this secret?
beginner.explanation: The Philistines bribed Delilah with silver to find out the source of Samson's strength. She nagged him repeatedly until he finally told her his power came from his uncut hair, his Nazirite vow to God.

beginner.question: In the book of Ruth, a loyal daughter-in-law refused to leave her mother-in-law and said the famous words: 'Where you go I will go, and where you stay I will stay.' Who said this?
beginner.explanation: Ruth was a Moabite woman who married into an Israelite family. After her husband died, she refused to leave her mother-in-law Naomi, declaring her loyalty with these beautiful words and following Naomi back to Bethlehem.

beginner.question: Ruth's beloved mother-in-law, whom she followed back to Bethlehem out of loyalty and love, was named what?
beginner.explanation: Naomi was Ruth's mother-in-law. After both their husbands died, Naomi decided to return to Bethlehem. Ruth insisted on going with her, showing remarkable loyalty and devotion that is celebrated throughout the Bible.

beginner.question: In Bethlehem, Ruth married a kind and wealthy landowner, and they became the great-grandparents of King David. Who did Ruth marry?
beginner.explanation: Ruth married Boaz, a wealthy relative of Naomi's late husband. Boaz noticed Ruth's hard work and loyalty to Naomi and took her as his wife. Their son Obed was the grandfather of King David.

beginner.question: A woman named Hannah desperately wanted a child and prayed to God at the tabernacle. God answered her prayer, and she dedicated her son to serve God. Who was this son?
beginner.explanation: Hannah prayed so earnestly for a child that the priest Eli thought she was drunk. God answered her prayer and she gave birth to Samuel. True to her promise, she brought young Samuel to the tabernacle to serve God.

beginner.question: The Israelites demanded a king so they could be like other nations. Who was the very first king of Israel?
beginner.explanation: Saul was chosen as the first king of Israel. He was tall, handsome, and from the tribe of Benjamin. The prophet Samuel anointed him king after the people demanded a king to rule over them.

beginner.question: A young shepherd boy was chosen by God to be the next king of Israel after Saul. The prophet Samuel anointed this boy. Who was he?
beginner.explanation: God sent the prophet Samuel to the house of Jesse in Bethlehem to anoint the next king. After looking at Jesse's older sons, God chose the youngest — David, a humble shepherd boy — because God looks at the heart, not outward appearance.

beginner.question: The Israelite army faced a giant Philistine warrior who was over nine feet tall and challenged anyone to fight him. No soldier was brave enough — until a young shepherd boy volunteered. Who was this giant?
beginner.explanation: Goliath was a giant Philistine warrior who stood over nine feet tall and wore heavy bronze armour. For forty days he challenged the Israelite army, but everyone was too afraid to fight him until young David stepped forward.

beginner.question: Young David, the shepherd boy, defeated the giant Goliath. What simple weapon did David use?
beginner.explanation: David refused King Saul's heavy armour and instead chose five smooth stones from a stream and his shepherd's sling. He slung a single stone that hit Goliath in the forehead, and the giant fell face down on the ground.

beginner.question: Before becoming a great king, David worked as a young man tending animals in the fields. What was David's job?
beginner.explanation: David was the youngest son of Jesse and worked as a shepherd, tending his father's sheep in Bethlehem. His experience protecting sheep from lions and bears gave him the courage to face Goliath.

beginner.question: King David was known for playing music to soothe King Saul's troubled spirit. What instrument did David play?
beginner.explanation: David was a skilled harp player. When King Saul was tormented by an evil spirit, his servants recommended David. Whenever David played the harp, Saul would feel better and the spirit would leave him.

beginner.question: King David wrote many of the songs and poems found in a particular book of the Bible. Which book contains these songs of praise?
beginner.explanation: The book of Psalms contains 150 songs and poems, and David is credited with writing about half of them. These include famous passages like Psalm 23 ('The Lord is my shepherd') and many songs of praise, lament, and thanksgiving.

beginner.question: One of the most famous Bible passages begins: 'The Lord is my shepherd, I shall not want.' Which Psalm is this?
beginner.explanation: Psalm 23 is one of the most beloved and well-known passages in the entire Bible. Written by David, it compares God to a caring shepherd who provides, protects, and guides His people through even the darkest valleys.

beginner.question: David conquered a city and made it the capital of Israel. This city is still important today. What city did David make his capital?
beginner.explanation: David captured the fortress city of Jerusalem from the Jebusites and established it as his capital, calling it the City of David. Jerusalem has remained one of the most important cities in religious history ever since.

beginner.question: King David's son became the next king of Israel and was famous for being the wisest man who ever lived. Who was this wise king?
beginner.explanation: Solomon, David's son, became king after his father. When God offered him anything he wanted, Solomon asked for wisdom to lead the people well. God was so pleased that He gave Solomon not only great wisdom but also wealth and honour.

beginner.question: When God offered the new King Solomon anything he wanted, what did Solomon ask for?
beginner.explanation: Young King Solomon humbly asked God for wisdom and understanding to govern the people of Israel justly. God was so pleased with this unselfish request that He gave Solomon wisdom along with riches and honour he hadn't asked for.

beginner.question: King Solomon built a magnificent structure in Jerusalem that became the centre of worship for the Israelites. What did Solomon build?
beginner.explanation: Solomon built the first Temple in Jerusalem, a magnificent house of worship for God. It took seven years to build and was decorated with gold, cedar wood, and precious stones. The Ark of the Covenant was placed inside.

beginner.question: Two women came to King Solomon, both claiming to be the mother of the same baby. To discover the truth, what did Solomon suggest doing with the baby?
options: Cut the baby in two and give half to each
Give the baby to the older woman
beginner.explanation: Solomon wisely suggested cutting the baby in half to share between them. The real mother immediately begged him to give the baby to the other woman rather than harm it. Solomon then knew she was the true mother and gave her the child.

beginner.question: A famous queen travelled a long distance to visit King Solomon and test his wisdom with hard questions. Who was she?
beginner.explanation: The Queen of Sheba heard about Solomon's great wisdom and came from far away with a large caravan of camels, spices, gold, and precious stones. After asking him many difficult questions, she was amazed and said the reports hadn't even told her half.

beginner.question: The prophet Elijah challenged 450 prophets of a false god on Mount Carmel. He asked God to send fire from heaven to prove He was the true God. What was the name of the false god?
beginner.explanation: Elijah challenged the prophets of Baal on Mount Carmel. Despite their frantic prayers, Baal sent no fire. But when Elijah prayed, God sent fire from heaven that consumed the offering, the wood, the stones, and even the water in the trench.

beginner.question: The prophet Elijah did not die a normal death. Instead, God took him up to heaven in a dramatic way. How did Elijah leave earth?
options: He walked through a door of light
A chariot of fire carried him up in a whirlwind
beginner.explanation: Elijah was taken up to heaven by a chariot of fire and horses of fire, carried away in a whirlwind. His student Elisha witnessed this amazing event and picked up Elijah's cloak, inheriting a double portion of his prophetic spirit.

beginner.question: A young Jewish woman became queen of Persia and bravely saved her people from a plot to destroy them. Who was this courageous queen?
beginner.explanation: Esther was a young Jewish woman who became queen of Persia. When an evil official named Haman plotted to kill all the Jews, Esther risked her life by approaching the king uninvited to plead for her people, and she saved them.

beginner.question: Queen Esther's older cousin raised her as his own daughter and helped her save the Jewish people. What was his name?
beginner.explanation: Mordecai was Esther's cousin who had adopted and raised her. He discovered a plot to destroy the Jewish people and urged Esther to use her position as queen to save them, saying perhaps she was made queen 'for such a time as this.'

beginner.question: There was an Old Testament man famous for his patience and faith. He lost his wealth, his children, and his health, but he never cursed God. Who was he?
beginner.explanation: Job was a righteous man who lost everything — his livestock, his servants, his children, and even his health — yet he remained faithful to God. In the end, God restored Job's fortunes and gave him twice as much as he had before.

beginner.question: A faithful man named Daniel continued to pray to God three times a day even though a new law made it illegal. As punishment, the king reluctantly threw Daniel into a den of what animals?
beginner.explanation: Daniel's enemies tricked the king into making a law that anyone who prayed to anyone other than the king would be thrown into a den of lions. Daniel kept praying to God and was thrown in, but God sent an angel to shut the lions' mouths.

beginner.question: When Daniel was thrown into the lions' den for praying to God, what happened to him?
options: God protected him and the lions did not hurt him
He fought the lions and won
beginner.explanation: God sent an angel who shut the lions' mouths so they could not harm Daniel. The next morning, the king rushed to the den and was overjoyed to find Daniel alive and unharmed. Daniel's faith in God had saved him.

beginner.question: Three young Jewish men — Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego — refused to bow down to King Nebuchadnezzar's golden statue. As punishment, they were thrown into what?
beginner.explanation: King Nebuchadnezzar was furious that the three men refused to worship his golden statue and ordered the furnace heated seven times hotter than normal. But when they were thrown in, a fourth figure appeared with them and they were completely unharmed.

beginner.question: When Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego were in the blazing furnace, King Nebuchadnezzar was shocked to see a fourth person walking with them in the fire. How many men did the king see inside?
beginner.explanation: The king threw three men into the furnace but then saw four figures walking unharmed in the flames. The fourth figure looked like 'a son of the gods.' The three men came out without even the smell of smoke on them.

beginner.question: God told a prophet to go to the wicked city of Nineveh to warn the people, but instead the prophet ran away and boarded a ship going the opposite direction. Who was this disobedient prophet?
beginner.explanation: God told Jonah to go to Nineveh and warn them to repent, but Jonah didn't want to go because he disliked the Ninevites. He boarded a ship heading in the opposite direction, but God sent a great storm to stop him.

beginner.question: After Jonah was thrown overboard during a storm, God sent a great sea creature to swallow him. What kind of creature swallowed Jonah?
beginner.explanation: God sent a great fish (often called a whale) to swallow Jonah. Jonah spent three days and three nights inside the fish, where he prayed to God. Then God commanded the fish to spit Jonah out onto dry land.

beginner.question: How long was the prophet Jonah inside the belly of the great fish?
beginner.explanation: Jonah was inside the great fish for three days and three nights. During this time, he prayed to God and repented. God then commanded the fish to vomit Jonah out onto dry land, and Jonah finally obeyed God's command to go to Nineveh.

beginner.question: After Jonah finally went to Nineveh and warned the people that God would destroy their city, what did the people of Nineveh do?
options: They repented and turned from their wicked ways
beginner.explanation: To Jonah's surprise, the entire city of Nineveh — from the king to the poorest citizen — repented. They fasted, put on sackcloth, and turned from their evil ways. God saw their repentance and spared the city from destruction.

beginner.question: One of the Ten Commandments says you should honour (respect and obey) two people in your life. Who are they?
beginner.explanation: The fifth commandment says 'Honour your father and your mother.' This is the first commandment with a promise — that things will go well with you and you will enjoy long life in the land God gives you.

beginner.question: According to the Ten Commandments, what day of the week were the Israelites supposed to keep holy as a day of rest?
beginner.explanation: The fourth commandment says to 'Remember the Sabbath day and keep it holy.' The Israelites were to work for six days and rest on the seventh, just as God created the world in six days and rested on the seventh.

beginner.question: Moses sent twelve spies to explore the Promised Land of Canaan before the Israelites entered. Only two spies came back with a positive report. One was Joshua. Who was the other faithful spy?
beginner.explanation: Joshua and Caleb were the only two spies who trusted God enough to say the Israelites could conquer Canaan. The other ten spies gave a fearful report about giants in the land, which caused the people to lose faith.

beginner.question: Who was the sister of Moses who watched over him as a baby when he was placed in the basket on the Nile River?
beginner.explanation: Miriam was Moses' older sister. She stood at a distance and watched when the basket was placed in the Nile. When Pharaoh's daughter found the baby, Miriam cleverly offered to find a Hebrew woman to nurse him — and brought their own mother.

beginner.question: In the book of Genesis, what special item did Jacob's son Joseph receive from his father that made his brothers extremely jealous?
beginner.explanation: Jacob gave his favourite son Joseph a beautiful coat of many colours (or richly ornamented robe), which made his eleven brothers terribly jealous. Their jealousy eventually led them to sell Joseph into slavery in Egypt.

beginner.question: Joseph had dreams that seemed to predict the future. In one dream, what did Joseph see his brothers' sheaves of grain doing?
beginner.explanation: Joseph dreamed that his brothers' sheaves of grain bowed down to his sheaf. He also dreamed the sun, moon, and eleven stars bowed to him. These dreams predicted that his family would one day bow before him, which they eventually did in Egypt.

beginner.question: When Jacob heard that there was food in Egypt during the great famine, he sent his sons there to buy grain. How many sons did Jacob have in total?
beginner.explanation: Jacob had twelve sons: Reuben, Simeon, Levi, Judah, Dan, Naphtali, Gad, Asher, Issachar, Zebulun, Joseph, and Benjamin. These twelve sons became the founders of the twelve tribes of Israel.

beginner.question: Jacob had two wives who were sisters. He worked for their father Laban for many years to marry them. What were their names?
beginner.explanation: Jacob loved Rachel and agreed to work seven years for Laban to marry her. But Laban tricked him and gave him Leah first. Jacob then worked another seven years for Rachel. Together with their servants, they became the mothers of Jacob's twelve sons.

beginner.question: During one of the plagues of Egypt, thick darkness covered the land for three days. How long did this plague of darkness last?
beginner.explanation: The ninth plague brought total darkness over all of Egypt for three days. It was so dark that people could not see each other or move around. But remarkably, the Israelites had light in their homes.

beginner.question: One of the plagues of Egypt involved a massive swarm of insects that devoured every plant and crop. What kind of insects were they?
beginner.explanation: God sent a plague of locusts that covered the ground until it was black. They ate everything green that the hail had left — every plant and fruit tree. Pharaoh begged Moses to pray to God to remove them, but still he would not let the Israelites go.

beginner.question: After the Israelites crossed the Red Sea safely, they looked back and saw that the Egyptian army pursuing them had been destroyed. What happened to the Egyptian soldiers?
options: The waters came back together and drowned them
They turned back and went home
They got lost in the desert
beginner.explanation: When the Egyptian army chased the Israelites through the parted Red Sea, God caused the waters to come crashing back together, drowning Pharaoh's entire army — chariots, horses, and soldiers. Not one of them survived.

beginner.question: The book of Proverbs in the Bible contains wise sayings about how to live well. Most of Proverbs was written by which wise king?
beginner.explanation: King Solomon, known as the wisest person who ever lived, is credited with writing most of the book of Proverbs. These wise sayings cover topics like honesty, hard work, friendship, and the importance of fearing God.

beginner.question: When God spoke to Moses from the burning bush, Moses asked God what His name was. What name did God give?
beginner.explanation: God told Moses His name is 'I AM WHO I AM' (sometimes written as Yahweh or Jehovah). This name means God is eternal and self-existing — He has always been and always will be. Moses was to tell the Israelites 'I AM has sent me to you.'

beginner.question: Joseph was sold by his brothers to travelling merchants who took him to Egypt. In Egypt, Joseph was bought as a slave by an official. Who was Joseph's first master in Egypt?
beginner.explanation: Joseph was bought by Potiphar, the captain of Pharaoh's guard. God blessed everything Joseph did, and Potiphar put him in charge of his entire household. But Joseph was later falsely accused and thrown into prison.

beginner.question: On the seventh day, how many times did the Israelites march around the city of Jericho before the walls fell?
beginner.explanation: For six days, the Israelites marched around Jericho once each day. On the seventh day, God told them to march around the city seven times. After the seventh time, the priests blew their trumpets, the people shouted, and the walls came tumbling down.

beginner.question: When David went to fight the giant Goliath, he picked up smooth stones from a stream. How many stones did he choose?
beginner.explanation: David chose five smooth stones from a stream, though he only needed one to bring down Goliath. He put them in his shepherd's bag and went out to face the giant with just his sling and stones, trusting in God for victory.

beginner.question: King David had a very close friend who was the son of King Saul. Their friendship is one of the most famous in the Bible. Who was this loyal friend?
beginner.explanation: Jonathan, King Saul's son, became David's closest friend. Despite knowing that David would become king instead of him, Jonathan loyally protected David from his father Saul's jealousy and attempts to kill him.

beginner.question: Samson once killed a thousand enemy soldiers using an unusual weapon he found on the ground. What did he use?
beginner.explanation: Samson found a fresh jawbone of a donkey lying on the ground, picked it up, and struck down a thousand Philistine men with it. This was one of many extraordinary feats of strength God gave Samson.

beginner.question: In the creation story, God created all the sea creatures and the birds on which day?
beginner.explanation: On the fifth day, God created all the creatures of the sea and the birds of the air. He blessed them and told them to multiply and fill the waters and the sky. Land animals and humans were created the next day.

beginner.question: God gave Adam a job in the Garden of Eden even before Eve was created. What was Adam tasked with doing to all the animals?
beginner.explanation: God brought every animal and bird to Adam to see what he would name them. Whatever Adam called each creature, that became its name. This showed the special authority and responsibility God gave to humans over the rest of creation.

beginner.question: After Adam and Eve ate the forbidden fruit and realised they were naked, what did they use to try to cover themselves?
beginner.explanation: After eating the forbidden fruit, Adam and Eve suddenly felt ashamed of their nakedness. They sewed fig leaves together to make coverings for themselves. Later, God made garments of animal skin for them before sending them out of the garden.

beginner.question: What was the occupation of Abel, the younger son of Adam and Eve who was killed by his brother Cain?
beginner.explanation: Abel was a shepherd who tended flocks, while his older brother Cain was a farmer. Abel offered the best of his flock to God, and God was pleased with his offering. Cain's offering of crops was not regarded in the same way.

beginner.question: God told Abraham to leave his homeland and go to a new land that God would show him. What was the name of the land God promised to Abraham and his descendants?
beginner.explanation: God promised to give the land of Canaan to Abraham and his descendants. This Promised Land — roughly the area of modern-day Israel and Palestine — would become the homeland of the nation of Israel, Abraham's descendants.

beginner.question: When God tested Abraham by asking him to sacrifice Isaac, Abraham was about to obey when God stopped him. What animal did God provide as a substitute sacrifice?
options: A ram caught in a thicket
beginner.explanation: Just as Abraham raised his knife, an angel stopped him. Abraham looked up and saw a ram caught by its horns in a nearby thicket. He sacrificed the ram instead of his son, and God blessed Abraham for his great faith.

beginner.question: In which country were the Israelites slaves for hundreds of years before God sent Moses to free them?
beginner.explanation: The Israelites lived in Egypt for about 400 years. Initially they were welcomed because of Joseph, but eventually a new Pharaoh enslaved them and forced them into harsh labour making bricks and building cities.

beginner.question: What cruel order did the Pharaoh of Egypt give that forced Moses' mother to hide him in a basket on the Nile?
options: All Hebrew men must join the army
All Hebrew families must leave Egypt
All Hebrew girls must be enslaved
All Hebrew baby boys must be thrown into the Nile
beginner.explanation: Pharaoh was afraid the Israelites were growing too numerous and powerful, so he ordered that every newborn Hebrew boy be thrown into the Nile River. Moses' mother hid him for three months before placing him in a waterproof basket on the river.

beginner.question: Before Moses confronted Pharaoh, he had fled Egypt and was living as a shepherd in what land?
beginner.explanation: After killing an Egyptian who was beating a Hebrew slave, Moses fled to the land of Midian. There he married Zipporah, became a shepherd for her father Jethro, and lived for forty years before God called him from the burning bush.

beginner.question: During the Exodus from Egypt, God guided the Israelites by day and by night with two miraculous signs. What were they?
options: An angel walking ahead of them
A pillar of cloud by day and a pillar of fire by night
A bright star and a rainbow
beginner.explanation: God led the Israelites through the wilderness with a pillar of cloud during the day to guide their way, and a pillar of fire at night to give them light. These pillars showed God's constant presence and protection on their journey.

beginner.question: While the Israelites wandered in the desert, God provided meat for them to eat along with the manna. What kind of birds did God send?
beginner.explanation: When the Israelites complained about only having manna and craved meat, God sent huge flocks of quail that covered the camp. The people gathered and ate the quail, satisfying their desire for meat in the wilderness.

beginner.question: Which Old Testament book begins with the words: 'In the beginning God created the heavens and the earth'?
beginner.explanation: Genesis is the first book of the Bible, and it opens with the famous words about God creating everything. The name Genesis itself means 'beginning' or 'origin,' and the book tells the story of creation and the early history of humanity.

beginner.question: The Israelites kept the Ark of the Covenant, a special golden chest, as the holiest object in their worship. What was kept inside the Ark?
options: The stone tablets of the Ten Commandments
beginner.explanation: The Ark of the Covenant contained the two stone tablets with the Ten Commandments that God gave to Moses. It also held Aaron's staff that had budded and a pot of manna. The Ark represented God's presence among His people.

beginner.question: After Noah's flood, on which mountain range did Noah's ark finally come to rest?
beginner.explanation: After the floodwaters receded, Noah's ark came to rest on the mountains of Ararat. Noah then sent out birds to check whether the land was dry enough to leave the ark, and eventually God told him it was safe to go out.

beginner.question: Samson was set apart for God from birth. His special status meant he was never supposed to cut his hair. What was this type of special vow called?
beginner.explanation: Samson was a Nazirite, set apart for God from before he was born. The Nazirite vow included not cutting one's hair, not drinking wine, and not touching a dead body. Samson's uncut hair was the visible sign of his commitment to God.

beginner.question: In the story of David and Goliath, the giant Goliath was a warrior from which enemy nation?
beginner.explanation: Goliath was a champion warrior of the Philistines, the longtime enemies of Israel. He came from the city of Gath. The Philistines proposed that a single combat between their champion and an Israelite would decide the battle.

beginner.question: King Solomon was the son of King David. Who was Solomon's mother?
beginner.explanation: Solomon's mother was Bathsheba. David had seen her from his rooftop and desired her, leading to one of the most famous stories of sin in the Bible. Despite the troubled beginning, Solomon was chosen by God to succeed David as king.

beginner.question: When Moses threw down his staff in front of Pharaoh, it miraculously turned into what?
beginner.explanation: God gave Moses a sign to show Pharaoh — when Moses threw his staff on the ground, it turned into a snake. Pharaoh's magicians did the same, but Moses' snake swallowed theirs, showing God's superior power.

beginner.question: In the Old Testament, there is a female judge who led Israel into battle and helped defeat the Canaanite army. Who was she?
beginner.explanation: Deborah was a prophetess and judge of Israel. She summoned the commander Barak and told him God would give him victory over the Canaanite general Sisera. She went with him into battle, and Israel won a great victory.

beginner.question: God promised that Abraham's descendants would be like the stars of the sky in number. What does the name 'Abraham' mean?
beginner.explanation: God changed Abram's name to Abraham, which means 'father of many nations.' This new name reflected God's promise that Abraham would be the ancestor of many peoples and nations, not just one family.

beginner.question: Joseph's brothers told their father Jacob that Joseph had been killed. What evidence did they show Jacob?
options: Joseph's coat dipped in goat's blood
beginner.explanation: After selling Joseph to merchants, his brothers took his beautiful coat, dipped it in goat's blood, and showed it to their father Jacob. The heartbroken Jacob believed a wild animal had torn Joseph apart and mourned for many days.

beginner.question: What was Moses' reaction when God told him at the burning bush to go speak to Pharaoh?
options: He said he would go the next day
He was afraid and made excuses, saying he was not a good speaker
He was excited and went immediately
beginner.explanation: Moses was reluctant and afraid. He told God he was not eloquent and was slow of speech. He even begged God to send someone else. God was patient but firm, and appointed Aaron as Moses' spokesman to help him.

beginner.question: The story of creation says God separated the waters above from the waters below on the second day. What did God call the space He created between them?
beginner.explanation: On the second day, God made an expanse to separate the waters and called it 'sky' or 'heavens.' This created the atmosphere between the waters above (clouds) and the waters below (seas).

beginner.question: On the third day of creation, God gathered the waters into one place and what appeared?
beginner.explanation: God commanded the waters to gather together so that dry ground appeared. God called the dry ground 'land' and the gathered waters 'seas.' Then God created plants and trees on the land, each bearing fruit with seeds.

beginner.question: Abraham had another son before Isaac was born. This son was born to Sarah's servant Hagar. What was his name?
beginner.explanation: Because Sarah could not have children initially, she gave Abraham her servant Hagar as a wife. Hagar bore a son named Ishmael, who is considered the ancestor of the Arab peoples. Isaac was born later to Sarah as God had promised.

beginner.question: Before God changed Jacob's name to Israel, Jacob was known for being what kind of person?
beginner.explanation: The name Jacob means 'he grasps the heel' or 'deceiver.' Jacob tricked his brother Esau out of his birthright and deceived his father Isaac to steal the firstborn's blessing. Later, God transformed him and gave him the name Israel.

beginner.question: What was the name of the father of the twelve sons who became the twelve tribes of Israel?
beginner.explanation: Jacob (later renamed Israel) had twelve sons who became the founders of the twelve tribes of Israel. The nation of Israel got its name from Jacob's new name, and each tribe was named after one of his sons.

beginner.question: Which prophet of God was fed by ravens when he was hiding by a brook during a drought?
beginner.explanation: God told Elijah to hide by the Brook Cherith during a drought. Ravens brought Elijah bread and meat every morning and evening, and he drank from the brook. This showed God's miraculous provision for those who serve Him.

beginner.question: The Philistines captured the Ark of the Covenant and placed it in the temple of their god. Terrible things began to happen to them. What was their god's name?
beginner.explanation: The Philistines put the Ark in the temple of Dagon, but the next morning the statue of Dagon had fallen face down before the Ark. They set it up again, but it fell and broke. The Philistines were also struck with plagues until they returned the Ark.

beginner.question: In the story of Daniel, King Nebuchadnezzar of Babylon conquered Jerusalem and took many young Jewish men to serve in his court. Daniel and his three friends refused to eat the king's food. What did they ask to eat instead?
beginner.explanation: Daniel and his friends asked to eat only vegetables and drink only water instead of the king's rich food and wine, which would have violated their Jewish dietary laws. After ten days, they looked healthier than all the others who ate the royal food.

beginner.question: According to the Bible, God created the heavens and the earth.
beginner.explanation: The very first verse of the Bible states: 'In the beginning God created the heavens and the earth.' This is the foundational belief of the Bible — that God is the Creator of everything.

beginner.question: According to Genesis, God created the world in ten days.
beginner.explanation: God created the world in six days, not ten. On the seventh day, God rested from all His work. Each day brought a different part of creation — light, sky, land, stars, animals, and finally humans.

beginner.question: The first thing God created, according to Genesis, was light.
beginner.explanation: On the first day of creation, God said 'Let there be light,' and there was light. God saw that the light was good and separated it from the darkness, calling them day and night.

beginner.question: According to the Bible, God rested on the seventh day after creating the world.
beginner.explanation: After six days of creating the heavens, the earth, and everything in them, God rested on the seventh day. He blessed the seventh day and made it holy, which became the basis for the Sabbath day of rest.

beginner.question: The first man created by God was named Moses.
beginner.explanation: The first man was named Adam, not Moses. God formed Adam from the dust of the ground and breathed life into him. Moses came much later in the Bible and was the leader who brought the Israelites out of Egypt.

beginner.question: Eve was the first woman in the Bible, created by God as a companion for Adam.
beginner.explanation: God saw that it was not good for Adam to be alone, so He created Eve from one of Adam's ribs. Eve was the first woman and became Adam's wife and companion in the Garden of Eden.

beginner.question: Adam and Eve lived in the Garden of Babylon.
beginner.explanation: Adam and Eve lived in the Garden of Eden, not Babylon. The Garden of Eden was a beautiful paradise created by God where every kind of tree grew. Babylon was an ancient city built much later in history.

beginner.question: A serpent (snake) tempted Eve to eat the forbidden fruit in the Garden of Eden.
beginner.explanation: The serpent was described as the most cunning of all the animals. It told Eve that eating the forbidden fruit would not cause death but would make her like God, knowing good and evil. Eve believed the serpent and ate the fruit.

beginner.question: After Adam and Eve ate the forbidden fruit, God allowed them to stay in the Garden of Eden.
beginner.explanation: After eating the forbidden fruit, God banished Adam and Eve from the Garden of Eden as a consequence of their disobedience. God placed angels with a flaming sword at the entrance to prevent them from returning.

beginner.question: God created Adam from the dust of the ground.
beginner.explanation: According to Genesis, God formed Adam from the dust of the ground and then breathed the breath of life into his nostrils, making him a living being. The name 'Adam' is related to the Hebrew word for ground or earth.

beginner.question: Eve was created from Adam's rib.
beginner.explanation: God caused Adam to fall into a deep sleep, took one of his ribs, and formed Eve from it. When Adam saw Eve, he declared she was 'bone of my bones and flesh of my flesh.'

beginner.question: Cain was Adam and Eve's younger son who tended sheep.
beginner.explanation: Cain was actually the older son, and he was a farmer who worked the soil. Abel was the younger son who was a shepherd and tended sheep. Cain killed Abel out of jealousy when God favoured Abel's offering.

beginner.question: Cain killed his brother Abel because he was jealous that God accepted Abel's offering.
beginner.explanation: Both brothers brought offerings to God — Cain brought crops and Abel brought the best of his flock. God favoured Abel's offering, which made Cain very angry and jealous, leading him to murder his brother.

beginner.question: Noah built a large ark to save his family and animals from a great flood.
beginner.explanation: God warned Noah that He was going to flood the earth because of humanity's wickedness. Noah obeyed God and built a massive ark, saving his family (eight people total) and pairs of every kind of animal.

beginner.question: Noah brought five of every kind of animal onto the ark.
beginner.explanation: Noah brought two of every kind of animal — one male and one female — onto the ark, not five. For certain clean animals used in sacrifices, he brought seven pairs, but the standard instruction was two of each kind.

beginner.question: It rained for 40 days and 40 nights during the great flood in Noah's time.
beginner.explanation: The Bible says rain fell on the earth for forty days and forty nights. The floodwaters rose and covered even the highest mountains, destroying all living things on earth except those safely inside Noah's ark.

beginner.question: After the flood, God placed a rainbow in the sky as a sign of His promise never to flood the entire earth again.
beginner.explanation: God made a covenant with Noah and all living creatures, promising He would never again destroy the earth with a flood. The rainbow was set in the sky as a visible reminder of this everlasting promise.

beginner.question: Noah sent an eagle to search for dry land after the flood.
beginner.explanation: Noah sent a dove, not an eagle. He first sent a raven, then a dove. The dove returned the second time with an olive branch in its beak, showing that the floodwaters were receding and plants were growing again.

beginner.question: The Tower of Babel was built by people who wanted to reach heaven and make a name for themselves.
beginner.explanation: After the flood, people gathered in one place and decided to build a city with a tower that reached to the heavens. They wanted to make a great name for themselves rather than spreading out across the earth as God intended.

beginner.question: God confused the languages at the Tower of Babel so the people could no longer understand each other.
beginner.explanation: God confused the language of the builders so they could no longer communicate with one another. Unable to work together, they abandoned the tower and scattered across the earth, forming different nations and language groups.

beginner.question: Abraham's wife was named Rachel.
beginner.explanation: Abraham's wife was named Sarah (originally Sarai), not Rachel. Rachel was a later figure — she was Jacob's beloved wife. Sarah was the mother of Isaac and is considered a matriarch of the Israelite nation.

beginner.question: God promised Abraham that he would be the father of a great nation.
beginner.explanation: God called Abraham to leave his homeland and promised to make him into a great nation, bless him, and make his name great. This promise was the beginning of God's covenant with Abraham and his descendants.

beginner.question: Abraham was willing to sacrifice his son Isaac when God tested his faith.
beginner.explanation: In the ultimate test of faith, God asked Abraham to sacrifice his son Isaac on a mountain. Abraham obeyed and was about to carry it out when God stopped him at the last moment and provided a ram as a substitute sacrifice.

beginner.question: Sarah laughed when she heard she would have a baby in her old age.
beginner.explanation: When three visitors told Abraham that Sarah would have a son, Sarah was listening from inside the tent. She laughed to herself because she was about 90 years old. The baby was named Isaac, which means 'he laughs.'

beginner.question: Lot's wife was turned into a pillar of salt when she looked back at the cities of Sodom and Gomorrah as God destroyed them.
beginner.explanation: God rained fire and brimstone on Sodom and Gomorrah, but allowed Lot's family to escape first. The angels warned them not to look back. Lot's wife disobeyed, looked back at the burning cities, and was turned into a pillar of salt.

beginner.question: Isaac was the son of Abraham and Sarah.
beginner.explanation: Isaac was born to Abraham and Sarah in their old age, exactly as God had promised. Abraham was about 100 years old and Sarah about 90 when Isaac was born. His birth was a miracle and a fulfilment of God's covenant promise.

beginner.question: Esau sold his birthright to his brother Jacob for a loaf of bread.
beginner.explanation: Esau sold his birthright for a bowl of lentil stew (red stew), not a loaf of bread. He came in from the field exhausted and hungry, and valued a single meal more than his rights as the firstborn son.

beginner.question: Jacob and Esau were twin sons of Isaac and Rebekah.
