beginner.explanation: Jacob and Esau were twins, with Esau being born first. They were very different — Esau was red, hairy, and loved hunting, while Jacob was quiet and preferred staying at home. Their rivalry shaped much of Genesis.

beginner.question: Jacob tricked his blind father Isaac into giving him the blessing meant for Esau.
beginner.explanation: With help from his mother Rebekah, Jacob disguised himself as Esau by wearing his brother's clothes and putting goatskins on his arms to feel hairy. The blind Isaac was fooled and gave Jacob the firstborn's blessing.

beginner.question: Jacob had a dream of a ladder reaching to heaven with angels going up and down on it.
beginner.explanation: While sleeping with his head on a stone, Jacob dreamed of a stairway or ladder reaching from earth to heaven, with angels ascending and descending. God stood at the top and renewed His covenant promise to Jacob.

beginner.question: Jacob's name was changed to 'Moses' after he wrestled with God.
beginner.explanation: Jacob's name was changed to 'Israel,' not Moses. After wrestling with a mysterious figure (believed to be God or an angel) all night, Jacob received the new name Israel, meaning 'he struggles with God.'

beginner.question: Jacob had twelve sons, and they became the founders of the twelve tribes of Israel.
beginner.explanation: Jacob (renamed Israel) had twelve sons: Reuben, Simeon, Levi, Judah, Dan, Naphtali, Gad, Asher, Issachar, Zebulun, Joseph, and Benjamin. Each son's descendants became one of the twelve tribes that formed the nation of Israel.

beginner.question: Joseph received a coat of many colours from his father Jacob because he was Jacob's favourite son.
beginner.explanation: Jacob loved Joseph more than his other sons because Joseph was born to Rachel, Jacob's favourite wife, in his old age. The special coat of many colours was a sign of this favouritism, which made Joseph's brothers very jealous.

beginner.question: Joseph's brothers loved him and were happy when their father gave him a special coat.
beginner.explanation: Joseph's brothers were not happy — they were extremely jealous and hated Joseph because their father favoured him. The special coat made their jealousy even worse, and they eventually plotted to get rid of him by selling him into slavery.

beginner.question: Joseph's brothers sold him into slavery, and he ended up in Egypt.
beginner.explanation: Joseph's jealous brothers threw him into a pit and then sold him to Ishmaelite merchants for twenty pieces of silver. The merchants took Joseph to Egypt, where he was sold as a slave to Potiphar, an officer of Pharaoh.

beginner.question: Joseph had the God-given ability to interpret dreams, which helped him rise to power in Egypt.
beginner.explanation: God gave Joseph the ability to interpret dreams accurately. He interpreted dreams for fellow prisoners and later for Pharaoh himself. Pharaoh's dreams about seven fat and seven thin cows predicted years of plenty and famine, and Joseph's interpretation made him second-in-command.

beginner.question: Pharaoh made Joseph the highest ruler in all of Egypt, with no one above him.
beginner.explanation: Pharaoh made Joseph second-in-command, not the highest ruler. Only Pharaoh himself was above Joseph. Pharaoh gave Joseph his signet ring, fine linen, and a gold chain, putting him in charge of all Egypt's affairs.

beginner.question: Baby Moses was placed in a basket on the Nile River to save him from Pharaoh's order to kill Hebrew baby boys.
beginner.explanation: Pharaoh ordered all newborn Hebrew boys to be thrown into the Nile. Moses' mother hid him for three months, then placed him in a waterproof basket among the reeds of the Nile. Pharaoh's daughter found him and raised him as her own.

beginner.question: Moses was found in his basket on the river by an Egyptian soldier.
beginner.explanation: Moses was found by Pharaoh's daughter, not a soldier. She came to the river to bathe, saw the basket among the reeds, and opened it to find the crying baby. She felt sorry for him and decided to adopt him.

beginner.question: God spoke to Moses from a burning bush that was not consumed by the flames.
beginner.explanation: While tending sheep in the wilderness, Moses saw a bush on fire that was not burning up. When he went to look, God called to him from the bush and told him to remove his sandals because he was standing on holy ground.

beginner.question: God sent five plagues on Egypt before Pharaoh let the Israelites go.
beginner.explanation: God sent ten plagues on Egypt, not five. These included water turning to blood, frogs, gnats, flies, livestock disease, boils, hail, locusts, darkness, and the death of the firstborn. Only after the tenth plague did Pharaoh let the people go.

beginner.question: The first plague of Egypt was God turning the water of the Nile River into blood.
beginner.explanation: Moses stretched out his staff over the Nile, and all the water turned to blood. The fish died, the river stank, and the Egyptians could not drink the water. Despite this dramatic sign, Pharaoh still refused to release the Israelites.

beginner.question: The last plague God sent on Egypt was a plague of locusts that ate all the crops.
beginner.explanation: The last and most devastating plague was the death of every firstborn son in Egypt — from Pharaoh's son to the firstborn of prisoners. The locust plague was the eighth plague, not the last one.

beginner.question: During the Passover, the Israelites put lamb's blood on their doorposts so the angel of death would pass over their homes.
beginner.explanation: God instructed each Israelite family to sacrifice a lamb and put its blood on the top and sides of their doorframe. When the angel of death saw the blood, he passed over that house, sparing the firstborn inside. This is the origin of the Jewish festival of Passover.

beginner.question: Moses parted the Red Sea so the Israelites could escape from the Egyptian army.
beginner.explanation: When the Israelites were trapped between Pharaoh's army and the Red Sea, God told Moses to stretch out his hand. The waters parted, creating a path of dry ground, and the Israelites crossed safely. The waters then collapsed on the pursuing Egyptian army.

beginner.question: The Egyptian army safely crossed the Red Sea behind the Israelites.
beginner.explanation: The Egyptian army did not make it safely across. When they followed the Israelites through the parted sea, God caused the waters to come crashing back together, drowning the entire Egyptian army — chariots, horses, and soldiers.

beginner.question: God provided manna — a special food from heaven — for the Israelites to eat in the desert.
beginner.explanation: When the Israelites were hungry in the wilderness, God sent manna each morning. It was a white, flaky substance that appeared on the ground like dew. The people gathered it daily, and it tasted like wafers made with honey.

beginner.question: The Israelites wandered in the desert wilderness for 20 years before reaching the Promised Land.
beginner.explanation: The Israelites wandered for 40 years, not 20. God made them wander for forty years because they lacked faith when the spies returned from Canaan. That entire generation (except Joshua and Caleb) died in the wilderness.

beginner.question: Moses received the Ten Commandments from God on Mount Sinai.
beginner.explanation: Moses climbed Mount Sinai, where God gave him the Ten Commandments written on two stone tablets. The mountain was covered in smoke and fire, with thunder and lightning, showing God's awesome presence.

beginner.question: God gave Moses seven commandments on Mount Sinai.
beginner.explanation: God gave Moses Ten Commandments, not seven. These included worshipping only God, honouring parents, keeping the Sabbath, and prohibitions against murder, theft, lying, and coveting. They were written on two stone tablets.

beginner.question: The Ten Commandments were written on two stone tablets.
beginner.explanation: God wrote the Ten Commandments on two tablets of stone. When Moses came down from the mountain and saw the people worshipping a golden calf, he smashed the tablets in anger. God later provided a replacement set.

beginner.question: While Moses was on Mount Sinai, the Israelites made a golden calf to worship.
beginner.explanation: While Moses was away on the mountain for forty days, the impatient Israelites pressured Aaron to make a god they could see. Aaron collected their gold jewellery and shaped it into a golden calf, which they worshipped with celebrations.

beginner.question: Moses made the golden calf for the Israelites to worship.
beginner.explanation: It was Aaron, Moses' brother, who made the golden calf — not Moses. The people pressured Aaron while Moses was on Mount Sinai. When Moses came down and saw the idol worship, he was furious and destroyed the calf.

beginner.question: Joshua was the leader who took over from Moses and led the Israelites into the Promised Land.
beginner.explanation: After Moses died, God chose Joshua as the new leader of Israel. Joshua had been Moses' faithful assistant for many years and one of the two spies who trusted God about conquering Canaan. He led the people across the Jordan River.

beginner.question: The walls of Jericho fell down after the Israelites marched around the city and blew their trumpets.
beginner.explanation: God gave Joshua unusual instructions: march around Jericho once a day for six days, and seven times on the seventh day with priests blowing trumpets. When the people gave a great shout on the seventh day, the massive walls collapsed flat.

beginner.question: The Israelites used battering rams to knock down the walls of Jericho.
beginner.explanation: The Israelites did not use any weapons or battering rams against Jericho's walls. God told them to simply march around the city, blow trumpets, and shout. The walls fell by God's miraculous power, not by any military siege equipment.

beginner.question: Samson's great strength came from God, and the sign of his special vow was his uncut hair.
beginner.explanation: Samson was a Nazirite — set apart for God from birth. His uncut hair was the outward sign of his vow to God, and as long as it remained uncut, God gave him supernatural strength to fight Israel's enemies.

beginner.question: Delilah was the woman who discovered and betrayed the secret of Samson's strength.
beginner.explanation: The Philistines bribed Delilah to find out why Samson was so strong. She nagged him repeatedly until he confessed that his power came from his uncut hair. She had his hair cut while he slept, and his strength left him.

beginner.question: Samson lost his great strength when his beard was shaved off.
beginner.explanation: Samson lost his strength when his hair was cut, not his beard. His long uncut hair was the sign of his Nazirite vow to God. When Delilah had his head shaved while he slept, the Lord's strength departed from him.

beginner.question: Ruth told her mother-in-law Naomi: 'Where you go I will go, and where you stay I will stay.'
beginner.explanation: After both their husbands died, Naomi decided to return to Bethlehem and urged Ruth to go back to her own people. But Ruth refused to leave Naomi, speaking these beautiful words of loyalty that are among the most quoted in the Bible.

beginner.question: Naomi was Ruth's sister.
beginner.explanation: Naomi was Ruth's mother-in-law, not her sister. Ruth married Naomi's son, and after he died, Ruth chose to stay with Naomi out of love and loyalty rather than return to her own family in Moab.

beginner.question: Ruth married a man named Boaz, and they became ancestors of King David.
beginner.explanation: Ruth married Boaz, a kind and wealthy landowner in Bethlehem. Their son was Obed, Obed's son was Jesse, and Jesse's son was David — the great king of Israel. Ruth the Moabite is honoured in the genealogy of King David.

beginner.question: Hannah prayed to God for a child, and God gave her a son named Samuel.
beginner.explanation: Hannah was deeply distressed because she could not have children. She prayed so fervently at the tabernacle that the priest Eli thought she was drunk. God answered her prayer, and she gave birth to Samuel, whom she dedicated to serve God.

beginner.question: David was the first king of Israel.
beginner.explanation: Saul was the first king of Israel, not David. David was the second king. The Israelites demanded a king so they could be like other nations, and God chose Saul from the tribe of Benjamin. David came to the throne after Saul's death.

beginner.question: Saul was the first king of Israel, chosen by God and anointed by the prophet Samuel.
beginner.explanation: When the Israelites demanded a king, God instructed the prophet Samuel to anoint Saul as the first king. Saul was tall, handsome, and from the tribe of Benjamin. He reigned over Israel but later disobeyed God and lost the kingdom.

beginner.question: Before becoming king, David was a shepherd boy who tended his father's sheep.
beginner.explanation: David was the youngest son of Jesse from Bethlehem and worked as a shepherd. He was out tending sheep when the prophet Samuel came to anoint him as the future king. His shepherd experience gave him courage to face Goliath.

beginner.question: David killed the giant Goliath with a sword and shield.
beginner.explanation: David killed Goliath with a sling and a stone, not a sword and shield. He refused King Saul's armour and instead chose five smooth stones from a stream. He slung one stone that struck Goliath in the forehead and knocked him down.

beginner.question: Goliath was a giant Philistine warrior who challenged the Israelite army.
beginner.explanation: Goliath was a massive warrior from the Philistine city of Gath, standing over nine feet tall. For forty days, he challenged any Israelite soldier to single combat, but everyone was too terrified to face him — until young David stepped forward.

beginner.question: David played the flute to soothe King Saul when he was troubled.
beginner.explanation: David played the harp (lyre), not the flute. When King Saul was tormented by an evil spirit, David's skilful harp playing would calm him and the spirit would depart. David was known as a talented musician.

beginner.question: David wrote many of the Psalms in the Bible.
beginner.explanation: David is credited with writing about half of the 150 Psalms. He was a gifted poet and musician who wrote songs of praise, worship, lament, and thanksgiving. The famous Psalm 23 ('The Lord is my shepherd') is attributed to David.

beginner.question: Psalm 23, which begins 'The Lord is my shepherd,' was written by King Solomon.
beginner.explanation: Psalm 23 was written by King David, not Solomon. David, who had been a shepherd himself, compared God to a caring shepherd who provides, protects, and guides His people. It is one of the most beloved passages in the entire Bible.

beginner.question: King Solomon was known as the wisest man who ever lived.
beginner.explanation: When God offered Solomon anything he wanted, Solomon asked for wisdom to rule his people well. God was so pleased that He gave Solomon unmatched wisdom, along with riches and honour. People came from all over the world to hear his wisdom.

beginner.question: When God offered King Solomon anything he wanted, Solomon asked for great wealth.
beginner.explanation: Solomon did not ask for wealth. He humbly asked God for wisdom and understanding to govern the people of Israel. God was so pleased with this unselfish request that He gave Solomon both wisdom and the riches he hadn't asked for.

beginner.question: King Solomon built the first Temple in Jerusalem as a house of worship for God.
beginner.explanation: Solomon built a magnificent Temple in Jerusalem that took seven years to complete. It was covered in gold and cedar wood and became the centre of Israelite worship. The Ark of the Covenant was placed in the inner sanctuary.

beginner.question: When two women both claimed the same baby, Solomon wisely suggested cutting the baby in half to discover the real mother.
beginner.explanation: Solomon proposed cutting the baby in half to give each woman a share. The real mother immediately begged him to give the baby to the other woman rather than harm it. Solomon knew the one willing to give up the baby was the true mother.

beginner.question: Solomon was the son of King Saul.
beginner.explanation: Solomon was the son of King David, not King Saul. His mother was Bathsheba. Solomon succeeded his father David as king of Israel and is remembered for his great wisdom and for building the Temple in Jerusalem.

beginner.question: The Queen of Sheba visited King Solomon to test his wisdom with difficult questions.
beginner.explanation: The Queen of Sheba heard about Solomon's fame and travelled a great distance with camels loaded with spices, gold, and precious stones. She asked him many hard questions, and Solomon answered every one, leaving her breathless with admiration.

beginner.question: The prophet Elijah challenged the prophets of Baal on Mount Carmel, and God sent fire from heaven.
beginner.explanation: Elijah set up a contest: both he and the 450 prophets of Baal would prepare sacrifices, and whichever God answered with fire was the true God. Despite the prophets of Baal's frantic prayers, nothing happened. But when Elijah prayed, God sent fire that consumed everything.

beginner.question: Elijah went up to heaven in a chariot of fire and a whirlwind.
beginner.explanation: Unlike most people in the Bible, Elijah did not die a normal death. A chariot of fire and horses of fire appeared and separated him from his student Elisha, and Elijah was carried up to heaven in a whirlwind.

beginner.question: Elijah was taken to heaven on a cloud, carried by angels.
beginner.explanation: Elijah was taken to heaven in a chariot of fire and a whirlwind, not on a cloud carried by angels. His student Elisha witnessed this dramatic event and picked up Elijah's cloak, which symbolised receiving Elijah's prophetic ministry.

beginner.question: Queen Esther saved the Jewish people from a plot to destroy them while she was queen of Persia.
beginner.explanation: Esther was a Jewish woman who became queen of Persia. When the evil official Haman plotted to kill all the Jews, Esther risked her life by approaching the king uninvited. She revealed the plot and saved her people from destruction.

beginner.question: Mordecai was the villain who plotted against the Jewish people in the book of Esther.
beginner.explanation: Mordecai was not the villain — he was Esther's cousin who raised her and helped save the Jewish people. The villain was Haman, a high official who hated Mordecai and plotted to destroy all the Jews in the Persian Empire.

beginner.question: Job lost his wealth, his children, and his health, but he remained faithful to God.
beginner.explanation: Job was a righteous and wealthy man who lost everything — his livestock, servants, children, and finally his health — as a test of his faithfulness. Despite immense suffering, he never cursed God, and in the end God restored all he had lost and more.

beginner.question: Daniel was thrown into a den of bears for praying to God.
beginner.explanation: Daniel was thrown into a den of lions, not bears. His enemies tricked the king into making a law that forbade praying to anyone but the king. Daniel continued to pray to God and was thrown into the lions' den, but God shut the lions' mouths.

beginner.question: God protected Daniel in the lions' den, and the lions did not harm him.
beginner.explanation: God sent an angel who shut the lions' mouths so they could not harm Daniel. The next morning, the king rushed to the den and was overjoyed to find Daniel alive and well. Daniel's faith in God had saved his life.

beginner.question: Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego were thrown into a fiery furnace for refusing to worship a golden statue.
beginner.explanation: King Nebuchadnezzar built a golden statue and commanded everyone to bow and worship it. These three Jewish men refused because they worshipped only God. The furious king had the furnace heated seven times hotter and threw them in, but God protected them.

beginner.question: Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego were burned and injured in the fiery furnace.
beginner.explanation: They were completely unharmed. The king saw four figures walking in the flames (the fourth looked divine) and called them out. They emerged without a single burn, and their clothes and hair didn't even smell of smoke.

beginner.question: God told Jonah to go to the city of Nineveh and warn the people to repent.
beginner.explanation: God commanded the prophet Jonah to go to the great city of Nineveh and preach against its wickedness. However, Jonah did not want to go and instead boarded a ship heading in the opposite direction, trying to run away from God.

beginner.question: Jonah was swallowed by a great fish (whale) after being thrown overboard during a storm.
beginner.explanation: When Jonah's ship was caught in a terrible storm, the sailors threw him overboard at his request. God sent a great fish that swallowed Jonah whole. He spent three days and three nights inside the fish, praying to God.

beginner.question: Jonah was inside the great fish for seven days and seven nights.
beginner.explanation: Jonah was inside the great fish for three days and three nights, not seven. During this time he prayed to God. Then God commanded the fish to spit Jonah out onto dry land, and Jonah finally obeyed God's call to go to Nineveh.

beginner.question: When Jonah finally preached to the people of Nineveh, they repented of their wickedness and God spared their city.
beginner.explanation: The entire city of Nineveh — from the king to the poorest citizen — repented, fasted, and turned from their evil ways. God saw their genuine repentance and showed mercy by not destroying the city, much to Jonah's annoyance.

beginner.question: The book of Genesis is the first book of the Bible.
beginner.explanation: Genesis is the first book of the Bible, and its name means 'beginning' or 'origin.' It tells the story of creation, the first humans, the flood, and the patriarchs Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, and Joseph.

beginner.question: The book of Proverbs was mostly written by King David.
beginner.explanation: The book of Proverbs was mostly written by King Solomon, not David. Solomon was known as the wisest man who ever lived and collected wise sayings about how to live well. David wrote many of the Psalms instead.

beginner.question: Aaron was Moses' brother who helped him speak to Pharaoh.
beginner.explanation: Aaron was Moses' older brother. When Moses said he was not a good speaker, God appointed Aaron as his spokesman. Aaron helped deliver God's messages to Pharaoh and performed some of the miraculous signs in Egypt.

beginner.question: Miriam was Moses' mother who placed him in the basket on the Nile.
beginner.explanation: Miriam was Moses' sister, not his mother. Moses' mother (Jochebed) placed him in the basket on the Nile, while Miriam watched from a distance to see what would happen. Miriam cleverly helped arrange for their mother to nurse the baby.

beginner.question: David made Jerusalem the capital city of Israel.
beginner.explanation: David conquered the Jebusite fortress of Jerusalem and established it as his capital, calling it the City of David. This was a strategic choice because Jerusalem was centrally located and not associated with any particular tribe of Israel.

beginner.question: Jonathan was David's greatest enemy who constantly tried to kill him.
beginner.explanation: Jonathan was David's closest and most loyal friend, not his enemy. Despite being King Saul's son and next in line for the throne, Jonathan loved David deeply and repeatedly protected him from his father's jealous attempts to kill David.

beginner.question: The Israelites were slaves in Egypt before Moses led them to freedom.
beginner.explanation: The Israelites lived in Egypt for about 400 years and were eventually enslaved by a Pharaoh who feared their growing numbers. They were forced into hard labour, making bricks and building cities, until God sent Moses to deliver them.

beginner.question: God told Moses His name was 'I AM WHO I AM' when He spoke from the burning bush.
beginner.explanation: When Moses asked God what His name was at the burning bush, God replied 'I AM WHO I AM.' This name (related to Yahweh/Jehovah) expresses God's eternal, self-existing nature — He has always been and will always be.

beginner.question: When Moses threw his staff on the ground in front of Pharaoh, it turned into a snake.
beginner.explanation: God gave Moses this miraculous sign to demonstrate His power to Pharaoh. When Moses threw down his staff, it became a snake. Pharaoh's magicians did the same trick, but Moses' snake swallowed theirs, showing God's superior authority.

beginner.question: God guided the Israelites through the wilderness with a pillar of cloud by day and a pillar of fire by night.
beginner.explanation: During the forty years in the wilderness, God provided visible signs of His presence and guidance. A pillar of cloud led the way during the day, and a pillar of fire gave light at night, so the Israelites could travel at any time.

beginner.question: Moses struck a rock with his staff and oil came out for the Israelites.
beginner.explanation: When Moses struck the rock, water came out — not oil. The Israelites were desperate for water in the wilderness, and God miraculously provided water from a rock to quench the thirst of the entire nation and their livestock.

beginner.question: Joshua and Caleb were the only two spies who gave a positive report about the Promised Land.
beginner.explanation: Moses sent twelve spies to explore Canaan. Ten came back terrified, saying the people were too strong and the cities too fortified. Only Joshua and Caleb trusted God and said the Israelites could conquer it with God's help.

beginner.question: Deborah was a female judge and prophetess who led Israel in battle.
beginner.explanation: Deborah held court under a palm tree and judged disputes for the Israelites. She summoned the commander Barak and told him God would deliver the Canaanite army into his hands. She accompanied him into battle, and Israel won.

beginner.question: Samson killed a thousand Philistine soldiers with the jawbone of an ox.
beginner.explanation: Samson killed a thousand Philistines with the jawbone of a donkey, not an ox. He found a fresh jawbone lying on the ground, picked it up, and struck down a thousand men with this unusual weapon.

beginner.question: Noah's ark came to rest on the mountains of Ararat after the flood.
beginner.explanation: After the floodwaters began to recede, Noah's ark came to rest on the mountains of Ararat. Noah waited many more weeks before sending out birds and eventually leaving the ark when God told him the land was dry.

beginner.question: Adam was given the task of naming all the animals in the Garden of Eden.
beginner.explanation: God brought every animal and bird to Adam to see what he would name them. Whatever Adam called each creature became its name. This task showed the special authority and role God gave to humans over the rest of creation.

beginner.question: After eating the forbidden fruit, Adam and Eve covered themselves with palm leaves.
beginner.explanation: Adam and Eve sewed together fig leaves to make coverings, not palm leaves. After eating the fruit, they became aware of their nakedness and felt ashamed. God later made garments of animal skin to clothe them properly.

beginner.question: God promised Abraham that the land of Canaan would belong to his descendants.
beginner.explanation: God made a covenant with Abraham, promising that the land of Canaan would be given to his descendants as their homeland. This 'Promised Land' became the goal of the Israelites' journey out of Egypt under Moses and Joshua.

beginner.question: Abraham was originally from the city of Jerusalem.
beginner.explanation: Abraham was originally from Ur of the Chaldeans, a city in ancient Mesopotamia (modern-day Iraq), not Jerusalem. God called Abraham to leave his homeland and travel to the land of Canaan, which He promised to give to Abraham's descendants.

beginner.question: When God tested Abraham, He provided a ram as a substitute sacrifice instead of Isaac.
beginner.explanation: Just as Abraham was about to sacrifice Isaac, an angel stopped him. Abraham looked up and saw a ram caught by its horns in a nearby thicket. He sacrificed the ram instead, and God blessed him greatly for his obedience and faith.

beginner.question: Isaac's wife Rebekah was found by Abraham's servant at a well.
beginner.explanation: Abraham sent his trusted servant to find a wife for Isaac among his relatives. The servant prayed and met Rebekah at a well, where she showed great kindness by offering water to him and all his camels. She agreed to marry Isaac.

beginner.question: Esau was the younger twin who preferred staying at home.
beginner.explanation: Esau was the older twin, not the younger, and he loved the outdoors and hunting. It was Jacob who was the younger twin who preferred staying at home among the tents. The Bible says Esau was red and hairy at birth.

beginner.question: Jacob worked for Laban for seven years to marry Rachel.
beginner.explanation: Jacob fell in love with Rachel and agreed to work for her father Laban for seven years to marry her. The years felt like only a few days because of his love. But Laban tricked him and gave him Leah first, so Jacob worked another seven years for Rachel.

beginner.question: Joseph's brothers told their father that Joseph had been killed by a wild animal.
beginner.explanation: After selling Joseph into slavery, his brothers dipped his beautiful coat in goat's blood and showed it to Jacob. They let their father believe a wild animal had torn Joseph apart. Jacob was heartbroken and mourned for many days.

beginner.question: In Pharaoh's dream that Joseph interpreted, seven fat cows were eaten by seven thin cows.
beginner.explanation: Pharaoh dreamed of seven fat, healthy cows grazing by the Nile, then seven thin, ugly cows came and swallowed them up. Joseph explained this meant seven years of abundance would be followed by seven years of severe famine.

beginner.question: Joseph predicted three years of famine from Pharaoh's dreams.
beginner.explanation: Joseph predicted seven years of famine, not three. Pharaoh's dream about seven thin cows swallowing seven fat cows represented seven years of plenty followed by seven years of severe famine. Joseph advised storing grain during the good years.

beginner.question: Moses grew up in the royal palace of Egypt as the adopted son of Pharaoh's daughter.
beginner.explanation: After Pharaoh's daughter found baby Moses in the basket, she raised him as her own son in the Egyptian palace. Moses grew up with all the privileges of Egyptian royalty, receiving the best education and training available.

beginner.question: Moses was eager and excited when God told him to go speak to Pharaoh.
beginner.explanation: Moses was actually very reluctant and afraid. He made excuses, saying he was not a good speaker. He even begged God to send someone else. God was patient but firm, and appointed Aaron as Moses' spokesman to help him.

beginner.question: One of the Ten Commandments says to honour your father and mother.
beginner.explanation: The fifth commandment instructs people to honour (respect and obey) their parents. It is the first commandment that comes with a promise — that things will go well and you will enjoy long life. This commandment has been central to Jewish and Christian ethics.

beginner.question: The Ark of the Covenant contained the stone tablets with the Ten Commandments.
beginner.explanation: The Ark of the Covenant was a gold-covered wooden chest that contained the two stone tablets on which God had written the Ten Commandments. It represented God's presence among His people and was kept in the holiest part of the Tabernacle.

beginner.question: The Israelites crossed the Jordan River on foot, not the Red Sea, to enter the Promised Land under Joshua.
beginner.explanation: While Moses parted the Red Sea to escape Egypt, it was the Jordan River that the Israelites crossed under Joshua's leadership to enter the Promised Land. God stopped the river's flow so they could walk across on dry ground.

beginner.question: On the seventh day at Jericho, the Israelites marched around the city seven times.
beginner.explanation: For six days, the Israelites marched around Jericho once each day. On the seventh day, God instructed them to march around the city seven times. After the seventh circuit, the priests blew their trumpets, the people shouted, and the walls collapsed.

beginner.question: Abraham's original name was 'Abram' before God changed it.
beginner.explanation: God changed Abram's name to Abraham, which means 'father of many nations.' This name change was part of God's covenant promise that Abraham would have countless descendants, even though he was already very old at the time.

beginner.question: Ishmael was the son of Abraham and his wife Sarah.
beginner.explanation: Ishmael was the son of Abraham and Hagar, Sarah's Egyptian servant. Because Sarah could not have children at first, she gave Hagar to Abraham. Isaac was the son of Abraham and Sarah, born later as God had promised.

beginner.question: The name 'Israel' was given to Jacob after he wrestled with God.
beginner.explanation: Jacob wrestled with a mysterious figure all night long. Before dawn, the figure changed Jacob's name to Israel, meaning 'he struggles with God.' The twelve tribes of Israel — the entire nation — took their name from Jacob's new name.

beginner.question: Joseph was Jacob's least favourite son.
beginner.explanation: Joseph was actually Jacob's favourite son, not his least favourite. Jacob loved Joseph more than his other sons because Joseph was born to Rachel (Jacob's favourite wife) in his old age. This favouritism, shown by the coat of many colours, caused his brothers' jealousy.

beginner.question: Moses fled to the land of Midian after killing an Egyptian who was beating a Hebrew slave.
beginner.explanation: When Moses saw an Egyptian beating a Hebrew slave, he killed the Egyptian and hid the body. When Pharaoh found out, Moses fled to Midian, where he lived as a shepherd for forty years before God called him from the burning bush.

beginner.question: One of the plagues of Egypt was a plague of darkness that lasted for three days.
beginner.explanation: The ninth plague brought total darkness over all of Egypt for three days. The darkness was so thick that the Egyptians could not see each other or go anywhere. Yet the Israelites had light in their homes in the region of Goshen.

beginner.question: The second plague of Egypt was a plague of snakes.
beginner.explanation: The second plague was frogs, not snakes. God sent frogs that came up from the Nile and covered the entire land — they were in the houses, bedrooms, ovens, and even the kneading bowls. Pharaoh begged Moses to remove them.

beginner.question: David picked up five smooth stones from a stream before facing the giant Goliath.
beginner.explanation: Before confronting Goliath, David went to a stream and selected five smooth stones, putting them in his shepherd's bag. Though he only used one stone to bring down the giant, he wisely prepared extras just in case.

beginner.question: King Saul was jealous of David and tried to kill him multiple times.
beginner.explanation: After David killed Goliath and became popular, Saul grew intensely jealous. He threw spears at David, sent him on dangerous missions hoping he'd be killed, and pursued him through the wilderness. David had to flee and hide for years.

beginner.question: The Israelites entered the Promised Land under the leadership of Moses.
beginner.explanation: The Israelites entered the Promised Land under Joshua's leadership, not Moses'. God did not allow Moses to enter the Promised Land because he had disobeyed God's instructions at the waters of Meribah. Moses died and was buried before the crossing.

beginner.question: Daniel and his three friends chose to eat only vegetables and water instead of the king's rich food.
beginner.explanation: When Daniel and his friends were taken to King Nebuchadnezzar's court, they refused the king's food and wine to honour their Jewish dietary laws. They asked for only vegetables and water, and after ten days they looked healthier than everyone else.

beginner.question: Daniel's three friends in the fiery furnace were named Peter, James, and John.
beginner.explanation: Daniel's three friends were Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego, not Peter, James, and John. Peter, James, and John are figures from the New Testament who were disciples of Jesus. The three friends were fellow Jewish captives in Babylon.

beginner.question: Jonah ran away from God because he did not want to preach to the people of Nineveh.
beginner.explanation: Jonah disliked the Ninevites (enemies of Israel) and did not want God to show them mercy. Instead of going to Nineveh as commanded, he boarded a ship heading the opposite direction, trying to flee from God's presence.

beginner.question: The people of Nineveh ignored Jonah's message and refused to repent.
beginner.explanation: The people of Nineveh actually listened to Jonah and repented. From the king to the common people, everyone fasted and turned from their evil ways. God saw their genuine repentance and spared the city from destruction.

beginner.question: The prophet Elijah was fed by ravens who brought him bread and meat while he hid by a brook.
beginner.explanation: During a drought, God told Elijah to hide by the Brook Cherith. Ravens brought him bread and meat every morning and evening, and he drank from the brook. This demonstrated God's miraculous provision for His faithful servants.

beginner.question: Solomon wrote most of the book of Psalms.
beginner.explanation: David wrote most of the Psalms, not Solomon. Solomon is credited with writing most of the book of Proverbs, the Song of Solomon, and Ecclesiastes. David was the poet and musician who composed about half of the 150 Psalms.

beginner.question: God created humans on the sixth day of creation.
beginner.explanation: On the sixth day, God created land animals and then humans — both male and female — in His own image. Humans were the final and crowning act of God's creation, given authority over all other living creatures.

beginner.question: The forbidden tree in the Garden of Eden was called the Tree of Life.
beginner.explanation: The forbidden tree was the Tree of Knowledge of Good and Evil, not the Tree of Life. Both trees were in the garden, but God specifically told Adam and Eve not to eat from the Tree of Knowledge of Good and Evil.

beginner.question: Noah had three sons: Shem, Ham, and Japheth.
beginner.explanation: Noah's three sons were Shem, Ham, and Japheth. All three, along with their wives, were on the ark during the great flood. After the flood, their descendants repopulated the earth and formed the different nations of the world.

beginner.question: The plague of hail was the second plague God sent on Egypt.
beginner.explanation: The plague of hail was the seventh plague, not the second. The second plague was frogs. The plague of hail was devastating — it destroyed crops, trees, and anything left in the open fields, along with thunder and fire that ran along the ground.

beginner.question: The Passover celebration remembers when God delivered the Israelites from slavery in Egypt.
beginner.explanation: The Passover is one of the most important Jewish holidays. It commemorates the night when the angel of death 'passed over' the Israelite homes that had lamb's blood on their doorposts, leading to the Israelites' liberation from Egyptian slavery.

beginner.question: God sent quail for the Israelites to eat when they craved meat in the wilderness.
beginner.explanation: When the Israelites complained about only having manna and wanted meat, God sent a massive flock of quail that covered the camp. The people gathered and ate the quail, satisfying their craving for meat.

beginner.question: King Nebuchadnezzar saw three people in the fiery furnace.
beginner.explanation: King Nebuchadnezzar saw four people walking in the flames, not three. He had thrown in three men — Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego — but was astonished to see a fourth figure who looked like 'a son of the gods.'

beginner.question: Esther's cousin Mordecai told her she may have been made queen 'for such a time as this.'
beginner.explanation: When Esther was afraid to approach the king uninvited (which could mean death), Mordecai told her that perhaps God had placed her in the position of queen specifically to save her people at this critical moment. This gave her the courage to act.

beginner.question: The first of the Ten Commandments is 'You shall not steal.'
beginner.explanation: The first commandment is 'You shall have no other gods before me,' not 'You shall not steal.' The commandment about stealing is the eighth. The first commandment establishes that God alone is to be worshipped.

beginner.question: Jacob worked a total of fourteen years for Laban in order to marry both Leah and Rachel.
beginner.explanation: Jacob worked seven years to marry Rachel, but Laban tricked him and gave him Leah instead. Jacob then had to work another seven years for Rachel — fourteen years total. Despite this, the Bible says the years seemed like days because of his love for Rachel.

beginner.question: In the creation account, God said 'It is not good for man to be alone' before creating Eve.
beginner.explanation: After creating Adam, God observed that it was not good for him to be alone and decided to make a suitable companion. God then created Eve from Adam's rib, establishing the first marriage and companionship in the Bible.

beginner.question: According to the Gospels, in which small town near Jerusalem was Jesus born?
beginner.explanation: Jesus was born in Bethlehem. Mary and Joseph had travelled there for a Roman census ordered by Caesar Augustus. Because the inn was full, Jesus was laid in a manger.

beginner.question: When baby Jesus was born, there was no room at the inn so Mary laid him in a feeding trough for animals. What is this feeding trough called?
beginner.explanation: Mary laid baby Jesus in a manger, which is a feeding trough for livestock. This is one of the most well-known details of the Christmas story, showing the humble circumstances of Jesus' birth.

beginner.question: Who was the mother of Jesus, chosen by God to give birth to His Son?
beginner.explanation: Mary was the mother of Jesus. The angel Gabriel appeared to her and told her she would conceive a child by the Holy Spirit. She was a young woman from Nazareth engaged to Joseph.

beginner.question: Who was the man engaged to Mary who raised Jesus as his earthly father?
beginner.explanation: Joseph was Mary's husband and served as Jesus' earthly father. He was a carpenter from Nazareth. An angel appeared to him in a dream and told him not to be afraid to take Mary as his wife.

beginner.question: Which angel appeared to Mary to tell her she would give birth to the Son of God?
beginner.explanation: The angel Gabriel was sent by God to Nazareth to tell Mary she had found favour with God and would conceive a son named Jesus. Gabriel also appeared to Zechariah about the birth of John the Baptist.

beginner.question: Why did Mary and Joseph travel from Nazareth to Bethlehem shortly before Jesus was born?
beginner.explanation: Caesar Augustus ordered a census of the entire Roman world, requiring everyone to return to their ancestral town. Joseph was from the line of David, so he travelled to Bethlehem, the city of David.

beginner.question: On the night Jesus was born, angels appeared to a group of workers in the fields nearby to announce the good news. Who were these workers?
beginner.explanation: Angels appeared to shepherds watching their flocks at night near Bethlehem. The angel told them not to be afraid and announced the birth of the Saviour. They went to find the baby lying in a manger.

beginner.question: After the angels told the shepherds about Jesus' birth, what did the angels sing?
options: Glory to God in the highest
beginner.explanation: A great company of heavenly angels appeared praising God and saying 'Glory to God in the highest, and on earth peace, goodwill toward men.' This joyful proclamation is one of the most famous parts of the Christmas story.

beginner.question: Visitors from the East followed a special sign in the sky to find the newborn Jesus. What did they follow?
beginner.explanation: The Wise Men (also called Magi) followed a star that appeared in the sky when Jesus was born. They travelled a great distance from the East, following the star until it stopped over the place where the child was.

beginner.question: The Wise Men brought three famous gifts to the young Jesus. Which of these was one of their gifts?
beginner.explanation: The Wise Men brought three gifts: gold, frankincense, and myrrh. Gold was a gift fit for a king, frankincense was used in worship, and myrrh was a costly spice. These precious gifts honoured Jesus as special.

beginner.question: Which cruel king tried to kill baby Jesus by ordering all young boys in Bethlehem to be killed?
beginner.explanation: King Herod was threatened by the news of a newborn 'King of the Jews' and ordered the killing of all boys aged two and under in Bethlehem. Joseph was warned in a dream and fled with Mary and Jesus to Egypt.

beginner.question: When King Herod tried to kill baby Jesus, where did Joseph take Mary and the child to keep them safe?
beginner.explanation: An angel appeared to Joseph in a dream and warned him to flee to Egypt with Mary and Jesus. They stayed in Egypt until Herod died, then returned to Israel and settled in Nazareth.

beginner.question: What was Joseph's occupation? The Bible tells us he worked with his hands building things from wood.
beginner.explanation: Joseph was a carpenter (or tekton in Greek, meaning craftsman). Jesus likely learned this trade growing up. People in Nazareth later referred to Jesus as 'the carpenter's son.'

beginner.question: In which town did Jesus grow up and spend most of his childhood?
beginner.explanation: Jesus grew up in Nazareth, a small town in the region of Galilee. After returning from Egypt, Joseph and Mary settled there. Jesus later became known as 'Jesus of Nazareth.'

beginner.question: When Jesus was a boy, his parents took him to Jerusalem for a festival. At what age did young Jesus amaze the teachers in the Temple with his understanding?
beginner.explanation: At age 12, Jesus stayed behind in the Temple in Jerusalem during the Passover festival, sitting among the teachers, listening and asking questions. Everyone who heard him was amazed at his understanding.

beginner.question: When Mary and Joseph found 12-year-old Jesus in the Temple after searching for him, what did Jesus say?
options: I must be in my Father's house
beginner.explanation: Jesus told his worried parents, 'Didn't you know I had to be in my Father's house?' This was one of the first signs that Jesus understood his special relationship with God, even as a child.

beginner.question: Before Jesus began his ministry, a prophet was baptising people in the Jordan River and preparing the way for Jesus. Who was this prophet?
beginner.explanation: John the Baptist preached in the wilderness and baptised people in the Jordan River as a sign of repentance. He proclaimed that someone greater was coming after him — Jesus, the Messiah.

beginner.question: In which river was Jesus baptised by John the Baptist?
beginner.explanation: Jesus was baptised in the Jordan River. He came from Galilee to the Jordan to be baptised by John. Although John felt unworthy, Jesus insisted it was the right thing to do.

beginner.question: When Jesus came up out of the water after being baptised, the Holy Spirit descended on him in the form of what?
beginner.explanation: After Jesus was baptised, heaven opened and the Holy Spirit descended on him like a dove. At the same time, a voice from heaven said, 'This is my Son, whom I love; with him I am well pleased.'

beginner.question: At Jesus' baptism, a voice spoke from heaven. Whose voice was it, and what did it say?
options: God said 'This is my beloved Son'
beginner.explanation: God the Father spoke from heaven saying, 'This is my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased.' This was a powerful moment confirming Jesus' identity as the Son of God at the very start of his ministry.

beginner.question: After his baptism, Jesus went into the wilderness to fast and pray. How many days did he spend there being tempted by the devil?
beginner.explanation: Jesus spent 40 days and 40 nights fasting in the wilderness, where he was tempted by Satan. Despite being hungry and weakened, Jesus resisted every temptation by quoting Scripture.

beginner.question: Who tempted Jesus during his 40 days in the wilderness?
beginner.explanation: The devil (also called Satan) tempted Jesus three times in the wilderness. He tempted Jesus to turn stones into bread, to throw himself from the Temple, and to worship Satan in exchange for worldly power.

beginner.question: During the temptation in the wilderness, the devil told Jesus to turn stones into what food?
beginner.explanation: After fasting for 40 days, Jesus was very hungry. The devil said, 'If you are the Son of God, tell these stones to become bread.' Jesus refused, saying, 'Man shall not live by bread alone.'

beginner.question: How many main disciples (also called apostles) did Jesus choose to follow him closely?
beginner.explanation: Jesus chose 12 disciples to be his closest followers and to continue his work. The number 12 was significant as it matched the 12 tribes of Israel. They travelled with Jesus throughout his ministry.

beginner.question: The first two disciples Jesus called were brothers who were fishermen on the Sea of Galilee. Who were they?
beginner.explanation: Simon Peter and his brother Andrew were the first disciples Jesus called. They were casting nets into the Sea of Galilee when Jesus said, 'Follow me, and I will make you fishers of men.' They immediately left their nets.

beginner.question: When Jesus called his first disciples, Simon Peter and Andrew were doing their job. What were they doing?
beginner.explanation: Peter and Andrew were fishermen casting their nets into the Sea of Galilee. Jesus told them to follow him and he would make them 'fishers of men.' They immediately left their nets and followed him.

beginner.question: Jesus called one of his disciples from a tax collection booth. Which disciple had been a tax collector?
beginner.explanation: Matthew (also called Levi) was sitting at his tax collector's booth when Jesus said, 'Follow me.' Matthew got up, left everything, and followed Jesus. He later wrote one of the four Gospels.

beginner.question: Jesus gave Simon a new name that means 'rock.' What was this new name?
beginner.explanation: Jesus renamed Simon as Peter, which means 'rock' in Greek (Petros). Jesus said, 'You are Peter, and on this rock I will build my church.' Peter became one of the most prominent leaders of the early church.

beginner.question: Jesus performed his very first miracle at a wedding celebration. In which town did this wedding take place?
beginner.explanation: Jesus' first miracle occurred at a wedding in Cana of Galilee. When the wine ran out, Jesus turned water into wine. His mother Mary had told him about the problem, and this miracle revealed his glory to his disciples.

beginner.question: At a wedding in Cana, the hosts ran out of wine. What did Jesus miraculously turn into wine?
beginner.explanation: Jesus told the servants to fill six stone jars with water, then told them to draw some out and take it to the master of the banquet. The water had turned into wine — and it was the best wine of the feast.

beginner.question: In one of Jesus' most famous miracles, he fed a crowd of thousands using just a small boy's lunch. How many people were fed?
beginner.explanation: Jesus fed about 5,000 men (plus women and children) with just five loaves of bread and two fish. After everyone ate and was satisfied, the disciples collected 12 baskets of leftovers.

beginner.question: To feed the 5,000, Jesus used a boy's small lunch. How many loaves of bread and fish did the boy have?
beginner.explanation: A young boy had five small barley loaves and two fish. Jesus took them, gave thanks, and miraculously multiplied them to feed the entire crowd of over 5,000 people with 12 baskets left over.

beginner.question: After feeding the 5,000, how many baskets of leftover food did the disciples collect?
beginner.explanation: After everyone had eaten their fill, the disciples picked up 12 full baskets of broken pieces of bread and fish. The abundance of leftovers showed the greatness of the miracle.

beginner.question: One night, the disciples were in a boat on the Sea of Galilee when they saw Jesus coming toward them. How was Jesus travelling across the water?
beginner.explanation: Jesus walked on the water of the Sea of Galilee to reach his disciples' boat during a storm. The disciples were terrified at first, thinking he was a ghost, but Jesus told them not to be afraid.

beginner.question: When Jesus walked on water, one brave disciple asked to come out to him on the water. He walked for a moment but then sank. Who was this disciple?
beginner.explanation: Peter stepped out of the boat and walked on the water toward Jesus. But when he noticed the strong wind and waves, he became afraid and began to sink. Jesus immediately reached out and caught him.

beginner.question: Jesus raised a man from the dead who had been in a tomb for four days. This man was a close friend of Jesus from the village of Bethany. What was his name?
beginner.explanation: Lazarus was the brother of Mary and Martha. When Jesus arrived, Lazarus had been dead for four days. Jesus wept, then cried out, 'Lazarus, come forth!' and Lazarus walked out of the tomb alive.

beginner.question: Before raising Lazarus from the dead, Jesus showed deep emotion. What is the shortest verse in the Bible, describing what Jesus did?
beginner.explanation: 'Jesus wept' (John 11:35) is famous as the shortest verse in the Bible. It shows Jesus' genuine grief at the death of his friend Lazarus, even though he was about to raise him from the dead.

beginner.question: The disciples were caught in a dangerous storm on the Sea of Galilee while Jesus was sleeping in the boat. What did Jesus do to stop the storm?
options: He told the wind and waves to be still
He steered the boat to shore
He woke the other disciples to help
beginner.explanation: Jesus stood up and rebuked the wind and the raging waters, saying 'Peace, be still!' The storm immediately calmed completely. The disciples were amazed, asking, 'Who is this that even the wind and waves obey him?'

beginner.question: Jesus healed ten men who had a terrible skin disease called leprosy. After being healed, how many came back to thank Jesus?
beginner.explanation: Only one of the ten healed lepers returned to thank Jesus, and he was a Samaritan. Jesus asked, 'Were not all ten cleansed? Where are the other nine?' This story teaches about gratitude.

beginner.question: Jesus gave a famous sermon from a hillside that contains some of his most well-known teachings. What is this sermon called?
beginner.explanation: The Sermon on the Mount is found in Matthew chapters 5–7. It includes the Beatitudes, the Lord's Prayer, the Golden Rule, and many of Jesus' core teachings about how to live a godly life.

beginner.question: Jesus taught his followers a model prayer that begins 'Our Father, who art in heaven.' What is this prayer commonly known as?
beginner.explanation: The Lord's Prayer was taught by Jesus when his disciples asked him how to pray. It includes praising God, asking for daily needs, seeking forgiveness, and asking for protection from temptation.

beginner.question: Jesus said, 'Do to others what you would have them do to you.' This famous teaching is known by what name?
beginner.explanation: The Golden Rule, found in the Sermon on the Mount, summarises how Jesus wanted people to treat each other. It means we should treat others with the same kindness and respect we want for ourselves.

beginner.question: In the Sermon on the Mount, Jesus began with a series of blessings such as 'Blessed are the poor in spirit' and 'Blessed are the peacemakers.' What are these blessings called?
beginner.explanation: The Beatitudes are a set of blessings that open the Sermon on the Mount. Each one begins with 'Blessed are...' and describes the qualities that God values, such as meekness, mercy, and purity of heart.

beginner.question: In the Beatitudes, Jesus said 'Blessed are the meek.' What did he promise the meek would receive?
beginner.explanation: Jesus said, 'Blessed are the meek, for they shall inherit the earth.' The meek are those who are humble and gentle. Jesus taught that true strength comes from gentleness, not aggression.

beginner.question: Jesus told a famous story about a man who was robbed and left wounded on the road. While others passed him by, a traveller from Samaria stopped to help. What is this parable called?
beginner.explanation: The Parable of the Good Samaritan teaches that our 'neighbour' is anyone in need, regardless of their background. A priest and a Levite walked past the injured man, but the Samaritan showed mercy and helped him.

beginner.question: Jesus told a story about a young man who took his inheritance, wasted it all, and then came home hoping to be a servant. Instead, his father threw a celebration. What is this parable called?
beginner.explanation: The Parable of the Prodigal Son illustrates God's unconditional love. When the wayward son returned home, his father ran to embrace him and threw a feast, showing that God rejoices when sinners return to him.

beginner.question: When someone asked Jesus, 'What is the greatest commandment?' what did Jesus answer?
options: Love the Lord your God with all your heart
beginner.explanation: Jesus said the greatest commandment is to 'Love the Lord your God with all your heart, with all your soul, and with all your mind.' He added that the second is to love your neighbour as yourself.

beginner.question: Jesus told a parable about a shepherd who had 100 sheep and one went missing. How many sheep did the shepherd leave behind to search for the lost one?
beginner.explanation: In the Parable of the Lost Sheep, the shepherd left his 99 sheep to search for the one that was lost. When he found it, he rejoiced greatly. Jesus used this to show how God cares for every individual.

beginner.question: Jesus compared the Kingdom of God to the smallest of all seeds, which grows into a large tree. What kind of seed did he use in this parable?
beginner.explanation: In the Parable of the Mustard Seed, Jesus said the kingdom of heaven is like a tiny mustard seed that grows into a large tree where birds can perch. It teaches that great things can grow from small beginnings.

beginner.question: Jesus said, 'I am the good _____. The good _____ lays down his life for the sheep.' What word fills the blank?
beginner.explanation: Jesus called himself 'the Good Shepherd' who lays down his life for his sheep. This imagery shows Jesus' love and willingness to sacrifice himself for the protection and salvation of his followers.

beginner.question: Jesus said, 'I am the way, the truth, and the _____. No one comes to the Father except through me.' What word completes the saying?
beginner.explanation: Jesus declared 'I am the way, the truth, and the life' to his disciple Thomas. This is one of the most well-known statements of Jesus, affirming that he is the path to God the Father.

beginner.question: Jesus said, 'I am the _____ of the world. Whoever follows me will never walk in darkness.' What word fills the blank?
beginner.explanation: Jesus declared, 'I am the light of the world.' He used the imagery of light versus darkness to describe the spiritual illumination he brings. Those who follow him are guided out of spiritual darkness.

beginner.question: Jesus taught about prayer, saying 'Ask, and it shall be given to you; seek, and you shall find; knock, and it shall be _____.'
beginner.explanation: Jesus encouraged persistent prayer with the promise that God hears and responds. 'Ask and it will be given to you; seek and you will find; knock and the door will be opened to you.'

beginner.question: Jesus told a parable about a wise man and a foolish man who each built a house. The wise man's house survived a storm because he built it on what?
beginner.explanation: The wise man built his house on the rock, so it stood firm when storms came. The foolish man built on sand, and his house collapsed. Jesus taught that those who obey his words have a foundation like rock.

beginner.question: Jesus said, 'You are the _____ of the earth,' telling his followers they add flavour and preserve goodness in the world. What word fills the blank?
beginner.explanation: Jesus told his disciples 'You are the salt of the earth.' Salt was used for preservation and flavouring. Jesus meant that his followers should have a positive, preserving influence on the world around them.

beginner.question: When Jesus saw children being brought to him, some disciples tried to send them away. What did Jesus say?
options: Let the little children come to me
beginner.explanation: Jesus said, 'Let the little children come to me, and do not hinder them, for the kingdom of heaven belongs to such as these.' He welcomed the children and blessed them, showing God's love for the young.

beginner.question: When Peter asked Jesus how many times he should forgive someone who wrongs him — up to seven times — what did Jesus reply?
beginner.explanation: Jesus told Peter to forgive not just seven times, but seventy times seven (or seventy-seven times), meaning there should be no limit to forgiveness. This teaches the radical and unlimited nature of Christian forgiveness.

beginner.question: In the Sermon on the Mount, Jesus taught 'Do not judge, or you too will be _____.'
beginner.explanation: Jesus warned that the standard we use to judge others will be applied to us. He used the illustration of trying to remove a speck from someone's eye while having a plank in your own eye.

beginner.question: In the Parable of the Sower, a farmer scattered seeds on different types of ground. Where did the seeds that grew and produced a great harvest fall?
beginner.explanation: The seeds that fell on good soil grew and produced a crop — some thirty, sixty, or a hundred times what was sown. Jesus explained that the good soil represents people who hear God's word and understand it.

beginner.question: A short tax collector named Zacchaeus wanted to see Jesus but could not see over the crowd. What did he do?
beginner.explanation: Zacchaeus climbed a sycamore-fig tree to see Jesus passing through Jericho. Jesus looked up and said, 'Zacchaeus, come down immediately. I must stay at your house today.' Zacchaeus was overjoyed.

beginner.question: On the day known as Palm Sunday, Jesus rode into Jerusalem while crowds cheered. What animal did Jesus ride?
beginner.explanation: Jesus rode a donkey into Jerusalem, fulfilling the prophecy of Zechariah. The crowds spread their cloaks and palm branches on the road, shouting 'Hosanna!' This humble entry showed Jesus came in peace, not as a warrior.

beginner.question: As Jesus entered Jerusalem on Palm Sunday, the crowd waved branches and shouted a word of praise. What did they shout?
beginner.explanation: The crowds shouted 'Hosanna to the Son of David! Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord!' Hosanna means 'Save us!' and was a shout of praise and hope that Jesus was the promised Messiah.

beginner.question: What did the people wave and lay on the ground as Jesus entered Jerusalem on Palm Sunday?
beginner.explanation: The crowd cut palm branches and spread them on the road, along with their cloaks, to honour Jesus as he entered Jerusalem. This is why the event is called 'Palm Sunday' and is celebrated by Christians today.

beginner.question: When Jesus found merchants buying and selling inside the Temple in Jerusalem, what did he do?
options: He overturned their tables and drove them out
He asked them to leave politely
beginner.explanation: Jesus overturned the tables of the money changers and drove out those selling animals for sacrifice. He said, 'My house shall be called a house of prayer, but you are making it a den of robbers.'

beginner.question: On a mountain, Jesus was transformed and his face shone like the sun and his clothes became dazzling white. What is this event called?
beginner.explanation: The Transfiguration was a miraculous event where Jesus' appearance was transformed on a high mountain. Moses and Elijah appeared and spoke with him, and God's voice from a cloud said, 'This is my Son; listen to him.'

beginner.question: At the Transfiguration on the mountain, two great figures from the Old Testament appeared and talked with Jesus. Who were they?
beginner.explanation: Moses and Elijah appeared with Jesus at the Transfiguration. Moses represented the Law and Elijah represented the Prophets. Together they showed that Jesus was the fulfilment of all Old Testament promises.

beginner.question: Which three disciples did Jesus take with him to witness the Transfiguration on the mountain?
beginner.explanation: Peter, James, and John were Jesus' closest inner circle. They were the same three he took with him to pray in the Garden of Gethsemane before his arrest. They witnessed many key moments of his ministry.

beginner.question: The night before his crucifixion, Jesus shared a final meal with his disciples. What is this meal known as?
beginner.explanation: The Last Supper was the final meal Jesus shared with his 12 disciples before his arrest and crucifixion. During this meal, Jesus broke bread, shared wine, and gave his disciples important teachings.

beginner.question: The Last Supper took place during which important Jewish festival?
beginner.explanation: The Last Supper was a Passover meal, commemorating when God freed the Israelites from slavery in Egypt. Jesus gave the Passover meal new meaning by connecting the bread and wine to his own sacrifice.

beginner.question: At the Last Supper, Jesus broke bread and gave it to his disciples. He said the bread represented what?
beginner.explanation: Jesus took bread, broke it, and said, 'Take and eat; this is my body.' He was showing that his body would be broken for them on the cross. Christians remember this in the practice of Communion.

beginner.question: At the Last Supper, Jesus shared a cup of wine with his disciples. He said the wine represented what?
beginner.explanation: Jesus said, 'This is my blood of the covenant, which is poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins.' The wine represented the blood he would shed on the cross as a sacrifice for humanity's sins.

beginner.question: At the Last Supper, Jesus performed a surprising act of humble service for his disciples. What did he do?
beginner.explanation: Jesus wrapped a towel around his waist, poured water into a basin, and washed his disciples' feet — a task usually done by servants. He taught them that true leadership means serving others.

beginner.question: Which disciple betrayed Jesus by telling the authorities where to find him?
beginner.explanation: Judas Iscariot, one of Jesus' 12 disciples, betrayed him. Judas went to the chief priests and agreed to hand Jesus over in exchange for 30 pieces of silver. He identified Jesus with a kiss in the garden.

beginner.question: How much money was Judas paid for betraying Jesus to the religious authorities?
beginner.explanation: Judas was paid 30 pieces of silver to betray Jesus. This was the price of a slave according to Old Testament law. After Jesus was condemned, Judas felt remorse and threw the money back into the Temple.

beginner.question: How did Judas identify Jesus to the soldiers who came to arrest him in the Garden of Gethsemane?
options: He shone a light on him
beginner.explanation: Judas identified Jesus with a kiss, saying, 'The one I kiss is the man; arrest him.' He approached Jesus and kissed him. Jesus replied, 'Do what you came for, friend.' This made the kiss a symbol of betrayal.

beginner.question: On the night Jesus was arrested, he went to pray in a garden. What is the name of this garden?
beginner.explanation: Jesus went to the Garden of Gethsemane, on the Mount of Olives near Jerusalem, to pray before his arrest. He was deeply troubled and prayed, 'Father, if it is possible, let this cup pass from me.'

beginner.question: In the Garden of Gethsemane, Jesus asked his disciples to do something while he prayed, but they failed. What did he ask them to do?
beginner.explanation: Jesus asked Peter, James, and John to stay awake and keep watch while he prayed. But they fell asleep three times. Jesus said, 'The spirit is willing, but the flesh is weak.'

beginner.question: When the soldiers came to arrest Jesus in the garden, one disciple drew a sword and cut off the ear of a servant. Who drew the sword?
beginner.explanation: Peter drew his sword and cut off the right ear of Malchus, the high priest's servant. Jesus told Peter to put away his sword, then he healed the servant's ear — showing mercy even during his arrest.

beginner.question: After Jesus was arrested, one disciple denied knowing him three times before a rooster crowed. Who was this disciple?
beginner.explanation: Peter denied knowing Jesus three times, just as Jesus had predicted at the Last Supper. When the rooster crowed, Peter remembered Jesus' words and wept bitterly. He was later forgiven and restored by Jesus.

beginner.question: Who was the Roman governor in charge of Jesus' trial who ultimately sentenced him to be crucified?
beginner.explanation: Pontius Pilate was the Roman governor of Judea. He questioned Jesus and found no basis for a charge against him, but under pressure from the crowd, he handed Jesus over to be crucified.

beginner.question: Pontius Pilate performed a symbolic action to show he did not accept blame for Jesus' death. What did he do?
beginner.explanation: Pilate washed his hands in front of the crowd, saying, 'I am innocent of this man's blood. It is your responsibility.' This gesture has become a well-known symbol of trying to avoid blame.

beginner.question: The crowd demanded that Pilate release a prisoner instead of Jesus. Who was this prisoner?
beginner.explanation: Barabbas was a notorious prisoner. Pilate offered the crowd a choice between releasing Jesus or Barabbas. The crowd, stirred up by the chief priests, chose Barabbas, and Jesus was sentenced to crucifixion.

beginner.question: Before his crucifixion, what heavy object did Jesus carry (or begin to carry) through the streets of Jerusalem?
beginner.explanation: Jesus carried his own cross toward the place of crucifixion. He was weakened from being beaten and scourged. A man named Simon of Cyrene was forced to help carry the cross part of the way.

beginner.question: What was placed on Jesus' head as a form of mockery before his crucifixion?
beginner.explanation: Roman soldiers twisted together a crown of thorns and placed it on Jesus' head, mocking him as 'King of the Jews.' They also put a scarlet robe on him and struck him. This was meant to humiliate him.

beginner.question: What is the name of the hill where Jesus was crucified, which means 'Place of the Skull'?
beginner.explanation: Jesus was crucified at Golgotha, an Aramaic word meaning 'Place of the Skull.' It is also known by the Latin name Calvary. This was a site outside the city walls of Jerusalem used for Roman executions.

beginner.question: How many other people were crucified alongside Jesus at Golgotha?
beginner.explanation: Two criminals were crucified with Jesus, one on his right and one on his left. One of the criminals mocked Jesus, while the other defended him and asked to be remembered. Jesus promised the repentant criminal paradise.

beginner.question: A sign was placed above Jesus on the cross. What did this sign say?
beginner.explanation: Pilate had a sign written in Hebrew, Latin, and Greek that read 'Jesus of Nazareth, the King of the Jews.' The chief priests objected, but Pilate replied, 'What I have written, I have written.'

beginner.question: While on the cross, Jesus spoke words of forgiveness. What did he say about those who crucified him?
options: Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they are doing
beginner.explanation: Even while suffering on the cross, Jesus prayed for those who crucified him: 'Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they are doing.' This shows the depth of his love and mercy even toward his executioners.

beginner.question: During the crucifixion, an unusual event happened in the sky for three hours. What was it?
beginner.explanation: From noon until three in the afternoon, darkness covered the whole land while Jesus hung on the cross. This supernatural darkness signified the gravity of what was happening — the Son of God dying for the world's sins.

beginner.question: When Jesus died on the cross, something dramatic happened in the Temple in Jerusalem. What was it?
options: The curtain was torn in two from top to bottom
beginner.explanation: The curtain of the Temple was torn in two from top to bottom. This massive curtain separated the Holy of Holies from the rest of the Temple. Its tearing symbolised that access to God was now open to all people.

beginner.question: A Roman centurion who witnessed Jesus' death was deeply moved. What did he say?
options: Truly this was the Son of God
beginner.explanation: The centurion guarding Jesus exclaimed, 'Truly this was the Son of God!' after witnessing the earthquake and the events surrounding Jesus' death. Even this Roman soldier recognised something extraordinary had happened.

beginner.question: After Jesus died, a wealthy follower asked Pilate for Jesus' body so he could give it a proper burial. Who was this man?
beginner.explanation: Joseph of Arimathea was a wealthy member of the Jewish council who was secretly a follower of Jesus. He asked Pilate for the body, wrapped it in linen, and placed it in his own new tomb cut from rock.

beginner.question: After Jesus' body was placed in the tomb, what was rolled in front of the entrance to seal it?
beginner.explanation: A large, heavy stone was rolled in front of the tomb's entrance. The chief priests also asked Pilate to post guards at the tomb, fearing the disciples might steal the body and claim Jesus had risen.

beginner.question: According to the Gospels, how many days after his death did Jesus rise from the dead?
beginner.explanation: Jesus rose from the dead on the third day after his crucifixion, just as he had predicted. He was crucified on Friday and rose on Sunday morning. This event is celebrated by Christians as Easter.

beginner.question: On Easter morning, who was the first person to discover that Jesus' tomb was empty?
beginner.explanation: Mary Magdalene went to the tomb early on Sunday morning and found the stone rolled away and the tomb empty. She was also the first person to see the risen Jesus, who she initially mistook for a gardener.

beginner.question: When the women arrived at Jesus' empty tomb on Easter morning, who told them 'He is not here; he has risen'?
beginner.explanation: An angel in bright clothing sat at the empty tomb and told the women, 'Do not be afraid. He is not here; he has risen, just as he said.' The angel told them to go tell the disciples the good news.

beginner.question: One disciple refused to believe Jesus had risen from the dead until he could see and touch Jesus' wounds. Which disciple was this?
beginner.explanation: Thomas (nicknamed 'Doubting Thomas') said he would not believe unless he could see the nail marks in Jesus' hands and put his hand into Jesus' side. When Jesus later appeared, Thomas believed and exclaimed, 'My Lord and my God!'

beginner.question: After his resurrection, Jesus appeared to two followers on the road to a village near Jerusalem. What was the name of this village?
beginner.explanation: Two disciples were walking to Emmaus when the risen Jesus joined them, though they did not recognise him at first. He explained the Scriptures to them, and they finally recognised him when he broke bread with them.

beginner.question: After rising from the dead, Jesus told his disciples to go and make disciples of all nations. What is this command commonly called?
beginner.explanation: The Great Commission is Jesus' instruction to his disciples: 'Go and make disciples of all nations, baptising them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit.' It is the foundation of Christian mission.

beginner.question: How many days did the risen Jesus appear to his disciples and others before ascending to heaven?
beginner.explanation: After his resurrection, Jesus appeared to his disciples and many others over a period of 40 days, teaching them about the kingdom of God. He then ascended to heaven from the Mount of Olives.

beginner.question: Jesus rose to heaven in front of his disciples. What is this event called?
beginner.explanation: The Ascension refers to Jesus physically rising into heaven 40 days after his resurrection. The disciples watched as he was taken up, and a cloud hid him from their sight. Two angels promised he would return.

beginner.question: After Jesus ascended to heaven, two angels appeared to the watching disciples. What did the angels tell them?
options: Jesus will come back the same way you saw him go
beginner.explanation: Two men in white (angels) said, 'Why do you stand here looking into the sky? This same Jesus, who has been taken from you into heaven, will come back in the same way you have seen him go.' This gives hope of Jesus' return.

beginner.question: Jesus healed a man who had been unable to walk for 38 years beside a pool in Jerusalem. What was the name of this pool?
beginner.explanation: At the Pool of Bethesda, Jesus healed a man who had been an invalid for 38 years. Jesus told him, 'Get up! Pick up your mat and walk.' The man was instantly cured and walked away carrying his mat.

beginner.question: Jesus healed a man who had been unable to see since birth. What was wrong with this man?
beginner.explanation: Jesus healed a man born blind by making mud with his saliva, putting it on the man's eyes, and telling him to wash in the Pool of Siloam. The man washed and could see for the first time in his life.

beginner.question: Jesus healed Peter's mother-in-law when she was sick. What was wrong with her?
beginner.explanation: Peter's mother-in-law was lying in bed with a fever. Jesus touched her hand and the fever left her immediately. She got up and began to serve them. This was one of Jesus' earliest recorded healings.

beginner.question: A woman who had been bleeding for 12 years believed she would be healed if she could just touch Jesus' clothing. What happened when she touched his garment?
options: She had to wait a week
