beginner.question: In the parable of the Leaven (Yeast), what did the small amount of yeast do to the large batch of dough?
options: It made the dough taste sour
It worked through the entire batch and made it all rise
It had no effect at all
beginner.explanation: Even though the yeast was tiny compared to the flour, it spread through the entire batch and caused all of it to rise. Jesus used this everyday baking image to show how God's kingdom starts small but permeates and transforms everything.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Leaven (Yeast), Jesus compared the Kingdom of Heaven to yeast. What is the main lesson of this short parable?
options: Yeast is the most important ingredient
Women are better bakers than men
Baking bread is a holy activity
The Kingdom of God may seem small at first, but it quietly spreads and transforms everything it touches
beginner.explanation: Like yeast silently working through dough, the Kingdom of God spreads quietly but powerfully, transforming lives, communities, and ultimately the whole world. You can't always see it happening, but its effect is unstoppable and all-encompassing.

beginner.question: Jesus was a master storyteller who used parables extensively in his teaching. What is a parable?
options: A short story that uses everyday situations to teach a spiritual or moral lesson
A type of prayer said in the temple
A list of rules to follow
beginner.explanation: A parable is a simple story drawn from everyday life — farming, cooking, family life, money — used to illustrate a deeper spiritual truth. Jesus used parables to make profound truths accessible and memorable for ordinary people.

beginner.question: Jesus told many parables during his ministry. According to the Bible, why did Jesus speak to the crowds in parables rather than giving straight explanations?
options: The crowds demanded stories instead of sermons
So that those who were seeking truth would understand, while those who rejected him would not
beginner.explanation: Jesus told his disciples that parables reveal truth to those who are open-hearted and seeking God, while concealing it from those who have hardened their hearts. He fulfilled the prophecy: 'I will open my mouth in parables.'

beginner.question: Many of Jesus' most famous parables are recorded in one particular Gospel more than any other. Which Gospel contains the most parables?
beginner.explanation: The Gospel of Luke contains more of Jesus' parables than any other Gospel. Many beloved parables — including the Good Samaritan, the Prodigal Son, the Rich Man and Lazarus, and the Pharisee and Tax Collector — are found only in Luke.

beginner.question: In Matthew chapter 13, Jesus told several parables in a row, one after another, all about the same subject. What was the common theme of these parables?
options: The Kingdom of Heaven — what it is like
beginner.explanation: Matthew 13 is sometimes called the 'Parable Chapter' because Jesus told multiple parables about the Kingdom of Heaven: the Sower, the Wheat and Weeds, the Mustard Seed, the Leaven, the Hidden Treasure, the Pearl, and the Net.

beginner.question: Jesus often began his parables with a particular phrase to introduce the story. What phrase did he commonly use?
options: The Kingdom of Heaven is like...
beginner.explanation: Jesus frequently introduced his parables with 'The Kingdom of Heaven is like...' or 'The Kingdom of God is like...' This phrase signals that the story is about to reveal something important about God's rule, his character, or how his kingdom works.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Good Samaritan, a Samaritan stopped to help a wounded man on the road after a priest and a Levite had both walked past without helping.
beginner.explanation: Jesus told this story to answer the question 'Who is my neighbour?' A priest and a Levite both saw the beaten man and crossed to the other side, but a Samaritan stopped, bandaged his wounds, and paid for his care at an inn.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Good Samaritan, the priest was the first person to stop and help the injured man on the road to Jericho.
beginner.explanation: The priest did NOT stop to help. He saw the wounded man and crossed to the other side of the road, walking right past him. It was the Samaritan — not the priest — who stopped and showed compassion.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Good Samaritan, the kind traveller poured oil and wine on the wounded man's injuries to treat them.
beginner.explanation: The Samaritan used oil to soothe the wounds and wine to clean them — common first-aid remedies in the ancient world. He then bandaged the man, placed him on his own donkey, and took him to an inn to recover.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Good Samaritan, the Samaritan left the wounded man on the road after bandaging his wounds and told him to walk to the nearest town.
beginner.explanation: The Samaritan did not leave the man on the road. He placed the injured man on his own donkey, took him to an inn, cared for him there, and even paid the innkeeper to continue looking after him. He went far beyond basic help.

beginner.question: Jesus told the parable of the Good Samaritan to teach that our neighbour is anyone in need, regardless of their background or nationality.
beginner.explanation: The parable was told in response to the question 'Who is my neighbour?' By making the hero a Samaritan — despised by the Jews — Jesus showed that true neighbourly love crosses all social, racial, and religious boundaries.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Good Samaritan, the injured man was travelling on the road from Bethlehem to Nazareth.
beginner.explanation: The man was travelling on the road from Jerusalem down to Jericho, not from Bethlehem to Nazareth. The Jerusalem-to-Jericho road was a steep, rocky, and notoriously dangerous route where robbers often attacked travellers.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Prodigal Son, the younger son asked his father for his share of the inheritance early, then left home and spent it all.
beginner.explanation: The younger son asked for his share of the estate, which was deeply disrespectful — essentially treating his father as if he were already dead. He went to a distant country and squandered everything on wild living until he had nothing left.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Prodigal Son, when the young man ran out of money, he got a job as a fisherman in a distant country.
beginner.explanation: The son did not become a fisherman. He was so desperate that he took a job feeding pigs — one of the most degrading jobs possible for a Jewish person, since pigs were considered unclean animals under Jewish law.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Prodigal Son, when the father saw his lost son returning home, he ran out to meet him and embraced him.
beginner.explanation: While the son was still a long way off, his father saw him, was filled with compassion, and ran to him — undignified for an older man in that culture. He threw his arms around him and kissed him, showing immediate and complete forgiveness.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Prodigal Son, the father turned his younger son away and made him work as a servant to earn his forgiveness.
beginner.explanation: The father did the opposite. He didn't make his son earn anything. He immediately gave him the best robe, a ring, and sandals, then threw a feast with a fattened calf. The son was fully restored, not punished or demoted.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Prodigal Son, the older brother was happy and joyfully joined the celebration when his younger brother came home.
beginner.explanation: The older brother was angry and refused to go inside to join the party. He resented that his wayward brother received such a lavish welcome when he himself had worked faithfully for years without getting a party.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Prodigal Son, the father ordered a fattened calf to be killed for the feast celebrating his son's return.
beginner.explanation: The father told the servants to bring the fattened calf and kill it so they could have a feast. He said 'For this son of mine was dead and is alive again; he was lost and is found.' The fattened calf was reserved for the most special celebrations.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Lost Sheep, a shepherd had 100 sheep and left the other 99 to search for the one that was lost.
beginner.explanation: The shepherd cared so much about the one missing sheep that he left the ninety-nine in the open country and went searching for it until he found it. This illustrates how God values every single individual and actively pursues those who are lost.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Lost Sheep, the shepherd decided that one lost sheep was not worth the trouble and stayed with the other 99.
beginner.explanation: The shepherd did not give up on the lost sheep. He left the ninety-nine and searched until he found the missing one. When he found it, he joyfully carried it home on his shoulders. Every single sheep mattered to him.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Lost Sheep, when the shepherd found the missing sheep, he joyfully put it on his shoulders and carried it home.
beginner.explanation: The shepherd was so happy to find his lost sheep that he placed it on his shoulders and carried it all the way back. He then called friends and neighbours to celebrate with him, saying 'Rejoice with me; I have found my lost sheep.'

beginner.question: In the parable of the Lost Sheep, Jesus said there is more rejoicing in heaven over one sinner who repents than over ninety-nine righteous people who do not need to repent.
beginner.explanation: Jesus drew a direct spiritual lesson from the story: heaven itself throws a party when even one lost person turns back to God. This shows the incredible value God places on each individual and his desire that no one be lost.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Lost Coin, a woman had 20 silver coins and lost one of them.
beginner.explanation: The woman had 10 silver coins, not 20, and she lost one. She lit a lamp and swept her entire house, searching carefully until she found it. Then she called her friends and neighbours to celebrate the recovery of that one coin.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Lost Coin, the woman lit a lamp and swept her house carefully to search for the missing coin.
beginner.explanation: Houses in that time often had dirt floors and no windows, making it very dark inside. The woman lit a lamp and swept every corner of her house, searching thoroughly until she found her precious coin. Her diligence reflects God's determination to find the lost.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Lost Coin, when the woman found her lost coin, she simply put it away and went about her day.
beginner.explanation: She did not quietly put it away. She was so overjoyed that she called together her friends and neighbours to celebrate with her, saying 'Rejoice with me; I have found my lost coin!' Her joy mirrors heaven's joy over a repentant sinner.

beginner.question: The parables of the Lost Sheep, the Lost Coin, and the Prodigal Son are all found together in Luke chapter 15 and share the same theme of God's joy when the lost are found.
beginner.explanation: Luke 15 contains all three 'lost and found' parables. Jesus told them in sequence to make the same point from different angles: whether it's a sheep, a coin, or a son, God searches for and celebrates the return of every lost person.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Sower, the seed that fell on the footpath grew into the strongest plants because the ground was firm.
beginner.explanation: The seed on the path did not grow at all. The ground was too hard for the seeds to take root, and birds came and ate them up. Jesus said this represents people who hear God's word but the devil snatches it away before they can believe.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Sower, the seed represents the word of God, and the different types of soil represent different ways people respond to God's message.
beginner.explanation: Jesus explained that the seed is God's word and the soils are people's hearts. Some hearts are hard like a path, some shallow like rocky ground, some crowded like thorny soil, and some receptive like good soil that produces an abundant harvest.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Sower, the seed that fell on rocky ground grew deep roots and produced a great harvest.
beginner.explanation: The seed on rocky ground did the opposite: it sprouted quickly but had no deep roots because the soil was too thin. When the sun came up, the plants were scorched and withered. This represents people who believe briefly but give up when difficulties come.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Sower, the thorns represent the worries of life, the deceitfulness of wealth, and desires for other things that choke out God's word.
beginner.explanation: Jesus explained that the thorns are life's distractions: worry, wealth, and worldly desires. These things crowd out God's word and prevent it from producing fruit, just as thorns choke a growing plant and stop it from bearing a crop.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Sower, the seed on good soil produced a crop of thirty, sixty, or even a hundred times what was sown.
beginner.explanation: The good soil produced an incredibly abundant harvest. Jesus said this represents people who hear God's word with an honest, good heart, retain it, and produce fruit through perseverance — their lives overflow with the results of faithful obedience.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Sower, all four types of soil produced an equally good harvest.
beginner.explanation: Only the good soil produced a harvest. The path soil produced nothing (birds ate the seed), the rocky soil produced a brief sprout that died, and the thorny soil produced plants that were choked. Only the good-hearted listener bears lasting fruit.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Mustard Seed, Jesus said the mustard seed is one of the smallest seeds, yet it grows into one of the largest garden plants.
beginner.explanation: Jesus chose the mustard seed specifically because it was the smallest seed familiar to his audience, yet it grew into an impressively large bush or tree. This contrast illustrates how God's kingdom starts from humble beginnings but grows to enormous proportions.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Mustard Seed, the tiny seed never grew very large — it stayed small just like the seed it came from.
beginner.explanation: The whole point of the parable is that the tiny mustard seed grows into a surprisingly large plant — big enough for birds to nest in its branches. It's a story about dramatic, unexpected growth from the smallest of beginnings.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Mustard Seed, birds come and perch in the branches of the fully grown mustard plant.
beginner.explanation: Jesus said the mustard seed grows so large that 'the birds of the air come and perch in its branches.' This image suggests that the Kingdom of God provides shelter, safety, and blessing for many, much like a great tree shelters birds.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Wise and Foolish Builders, the wise man built his house on sand and the foolish man built his on rock.
beginner.explanation: It was the other way around. The wise man built on rock (solid foundation) and his house survived the storm. The foolish man built on sand and his house collapsed. Rock represents obeying Jesus' words; sand represents hearing them but not obeying.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Wise and Foolish Builders, the house built on rock stood firm when the rain, floods, and winds came.
beginner.explanation: The house on rock withstood the full force of rain, floods, and howling winds because it had been built on a solid foundation. Jesus used this to illustrate the security of a life built on hearing and obeying his teachings.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Wise and Foolish Builders, both houses survived the storm because they were well constructed.
beginner.explanation: Only the house on rock survived. The house built on sand collapsed completely — it 'fell with a great crash.' The same storm hit both houses, but the outcome was completely different because of their foundations.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Wise and Foolish Builders, the wise builder represents a person who hears Jesus' words and puts them into practice.
beginner.explanation: Jesus said 'Everyone who hears these words of mine and puts them into practice is like a wise man who built his house on the rock.' The key difference between wise and foolish is not hearing — both hear — but whether they obey.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Talents, a master gave each of his three servants the same number of talents before going on a journey.
beginner.explanation: The master gave different amounts to each servant based on their ability: five talents to the first, two to the second, and one to the third. This shows that God entrusts people with different gifts and resources according to their capacity.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Talents, the servant who received five talents invested them and doubled his money to ten talents.
beginner.explanation: The servant with five talents put his money to work right away and gained five more, doubling the total to ten. When the master returned, he praised this servant saying 'Well done, good and faithful servant.'

beginner.question: In the parable of the Talents, the servant with one talent invested it wisely and earned a great return for his master.
beginner.explanation: The servant with one talent did NOT invest it. He was afraid of losing it, so he dug a hole in the ground and buried it. When the master returned, he was angry at this servant's laziness and fearfulness.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Talents, the master said 'Well done, good and faithful servant' to the servants who had multiplied their talents.
beginner.explanation: Both the servant who turned five into ten and the one who turned two into four received the same praise: 'Well done, good and faithful servant.' What mattered was not the amount but the faithfulness in using what they were given.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Talents, the master forgave the servant who buried his talent and gave him extra responsibility anyway.
beginner.explanation: The master did not forgive this servant. He called him 'wicked and lazy,' took away his one talent, and gave it to the servant who had ten. The unfaithful servant was cast out. The parable warns against wasting what God gives us.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Wedding Feast, a king prepared a wedding banquet for his son and invited many guests.
beginner.explanation: The king prepared a magnificent wedding feast for his son and sent out invitations to many guests. However, the invited guests refused to come and made excuses, so the king opened the invitation to everyone on the streets.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Wedding Feast, all the originally invited guests came eagerly and the banquet was a great success.
beginner.explanation: The originally invited guests refused to come. Some ignored the invitation, others went about their own business, and some even mistreated and killed the king's servants. The king then invited anyone from the streets to fill the banquet hall.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Wedding Feast, one guest was thrown out because he was not wearing wedding clothes.
beginner.explanation: When the king noticed a man without wedding garments, he asked how he got in without them. The man was speechless. The king ordered him to be bound and thrown outside. This represents accepting God's invitation without genuine heart change.

beginner.question: Jesus ended the parable of the Wedding Feast with the saying 'Many are invited, but few are chosen.'
beginner.explanation: This famous concluding statement means that while God's invitation to his kingdom goes out broadly to many people, not everyone responds with genuine faith and readiness. Accepting the invitation requires a real change of heart, not just showing up.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Rich Fool, a wealthy farmer had such an abundant harvest that he decided to build bigger barns to store all his grain.
beginner.explanation: The rich man's land produced so plentifully that his existing storage wasn't enough. His solution was to tear down his barns and build bigger ones. His focus was entirely on accumulating and keeping wealth for himself.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Rich Fool, the wealthy farmer decided to share his enormous harvest with the poor and needy in his community.
beginner.explanation: The rich man did NOT share anything. Instead, he planned to hoard everything for himself, build bigger barns, and then 'take life easy — eat, drink, and be merry.' He never once thought about God or helping others.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Rich Fool, God told the wealthy farmer 'You fool! This very night your life will be demanded from you.'
beginner.explanation: That very night, the rich man's life was demanded of him. All his stored-up grain and plans for an easy retirement meant nothing because he died unexpectedly. Jesus said this is what happens to those who store up wealth for themselves but are not rich toward God.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Rich Fool, the wealthy farmer lived a long and prosperous life and enjoyed all his stored-up treasure.
beginner.explanation: The rich man did NOT get to enjoy his wealth. God said 'This very night your life will be demanded from you.' He died that same night, and all his carefully stored grain and possessions went to others. His plans were completely futile.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Pharisee and the Tax Collector, the Pharisee prayed by listing all his good deeds and thanking God that he was better than other people.
beginner.explanation: The Pharisee stood up and prayed 'God, I thank you that I am not like other people — robbers, evildoers, adulterers — or even like this tax collector. I fast twice a week and give a tenth of all I get.' His prayer was full of self-congratulation.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Pharisee and the Tax Collector, the tax collector stood at the front of the temple and prayed loudly about his good deeds.
beginner.explanation: The tax collector did the opposite. He stood at a distance, wouldn't even look up to heaven, beat his chest in grief, and said only 'God, have mercy on me, a sinner.' His prayer was humble, short, and sincere.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Pharisee and the Tax Collector, Jesus said the humble tax collector — not the proud Pharisee — went home justified before God.
beginner.explanation: Jesus said the tax collector went home right with God, not the Pharisee. He concluded with the principle that 'everyone who exalts himself will be humbled, and he who humbles himself will be exalted.' God values honest humility over proud self-righteousness.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Pharisee and the Tax Collector, the Pharisee was justified before God because he followed all the religious rules perfectly.
beginner.explanation: The Pharisee was NOT justified despite his outward religious observance. His prayer was filled with pride and self-righteousness. Jesus taught that God looks at the heart, and the humble, repentant tax collector was the one accepted by God.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Unforgiving Servant, a king forgave his servant an enormous debt of 10,000 talents.
beginner.explanation: The king had compassion on the servant who owed 10,000 talents — an impossibly large sum worth millions in today's money. He cancelled the entire debt and let the servant go free, representing God's incredible grace and forgiveness.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Unforgiving Servant, after being forgiven his massive debt, the servant immediately forgave a fellow servant who owed him a small amount.
beginner.explanation: The forgiven servant did NOT show the same mercy. He grabbed a fellow servant who owed him only 100 denarii (a tiny sum compared to his forgiven debt), demanded payment, and had him thrown into prison. His hypocrisy was shocking.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Unforgiving Servant, the king punished the unforgiving servant after learning he had refused to show mercy to a fellow servant.
beginner.explanation: The king was furious and said 'Shouldn't you have had mercy on your fellow servant just as I had on you?' He then handed the unforgiving servant over to the jailers to be punished. Jesus warned that God will do the same to those who don't forgive from the heart.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Unforgiving Servant, the fellow servant owed the same enormous amount — 10,000 talents — that the first servant had been forgiven.
beginner.explanation: The fellow servant owed only 100 denarii — a tiny fraction compared to the 10,000 talents the first servant had been forgiven. This massive contrast highlights the hypocrisy of receiving enormous forgiveness but refusing to extend small mercies to others.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Good Shepherd, Jesus said 'I am the good shepherd' and that the good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep.
beginner.explanation: Jesus made this bold declaration in John 10:11. Unlike a hired hand who abandons the sheep when danger comes, the good shepherd willingly sacrifices his own life to protect them. This foreshadowed Jesus' death on the cross for humanity.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Good Shepherd, the hired hand bravely fights the wolf to protect the sheep, just like the good shepherd does.
beginner.explanation: The hired hand does NOT fight the wolf. Jesus said the hired hand 'abandons the sheep and runs away' because he doesn't truly care about them — he's only in it for the money. Only the good shepherd stays and protects the flock, even at the cost of his life.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Good Shepherd, Jesus said that his sheep recognise and follow his voice.
beginner.explanation: Jesus said 'My sheep listen to my voice; I know them, and they follow me.' This reflects the real relationship between Middle Eastern shepherds and their flocks — sheep truly do learn and respond to their own shepherd's unique voice.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Good Shepherd, Jesus described himself as the gate for the sheep, saying whoever enters through him will be saved.
beginner.explanation: Jesus said 'I am the gate; whoever enters through me will be saved. They will come in and go out, and find pasture.' In ancient sheep pens, the shepherd literally lay across the opening as the door, protecting the sheep with his own body.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Workers in the Vineyard, all the workers — whether they started at dawn or near the end of the day — received the same pay.
beginner.explanation: Every worker received one denarius, regardless of how many hours they worked. Those hired at the eleventh hour got the same as those who worked all day. The landowner's point was that his generosity was his right and not unfair to anyone.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Workers in the Vineyard, the workers who started early in the morning were happy that the latecomers received the same wage.
beginner.explanation: The early workers were NOT happy. They grumbled and complained, saying 'These who were hired last worked only one hour and you have made them equal to us who have borne the burden of the work and the heat of the day.'

beginner.question: In the parable of the Workers in the Vineyard, the landowner asked the grumbling workers 'Are you envious because I am generous?'
beginner.explanation: The landowner reminded the early workers that he had paid them exactly what was agreed. He then asked 'Don't I have the right to do what I want with my own money? Or are you envious because I am generous?' This illustrates God's sovereign grace.

beginner.question: The parable of the Workers in the Vineyard teaches that God only blesses those who have served him the longest.
beginner.explanation: The parable teaches the opposite: God's grace is generous and available to all, regardless of when someone comes to him. Whether early or late in life, God offers the same generous gift of salvation. His kingdom doesn't run on a merit system.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Ten Virgins, five wise bridesmaids brought extra oil for their lamps, while five foolish ones did not.
beginner.explanation: All ten bridesmaids had lamps, but only the five wise ones brought extra flasks of oil. When the bridegroom was delayed and their lamps began to run out, the wise ones had reserves while the foolish ones were left in the dark.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Ten Virgins, only the wise bridesmaids fell asleep while waiting for the bridegroom; the foolish ones stayed awake.
beginner.explanation: All ten bridesmaids — both wise and foolish — fell asleep while waiting for the delayed bridegroom. The difference was not who stayed awake, but who was prepared. The wise ones had brought enough oil; the foolish ones had not.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Ten Virgins, the bridegroom arrived at midnight, and the foolish bridesmaids found their lamps going out.
beginner.explanation: At midnight, the cry rang out: 'Here's the bridegroom! Come out to meet him!' All ten woke up, but the foolish virgins discovered their lamps were going out because they had no extra oil. They desperately asked the wise ones to share.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Ten Virgins, the wise bridesmaids generously shared their extra oil with the foolish ones so everyone could get in.
beginner.explanation: The wise bridesmaids did NOT share their oil. They said 'There may not be enough for both us and you. Instead, go to those who sell oil and buy some for yourselves.' While the foolish ones were away buying oil, the bridegroom came and they were locked out.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Ten Virgins, when the foolish bridesmaids arrived late and knocked on the door, the bridegroom said 'Truly I tell you, I don't know you.'
beginner.explanation: The door was shut, and when the foolish virgins returned and cried 'Lord, Lord, open the door for us!' the bridegroom replied 'Truly I tell you, I don't know you.' The opportunity had passed. Jesus used this to urge spiritual readiness.

beginner.question: The parable of the Ten Virgins teaches that believers should always be spiritually prepared because no one knows the day or hour of Jesus' return.
beginner.explanation: Jesus concluded with 'Therefore keep watch, because you do not know the day or the hour.' The parable warns that there will come a point when it's too late to prepare. Spiritual readiness must be maintained now, not left until the last minute.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Pearl of Great Price, a merchant found an extremely valuable pearl and sold everything he owned to buy it.
beginner.explanation: The merchant was searching for fine pearls and found one of extraordinary value. In his joy, he sold everything he had to purchase that single pearl. Jesus used this to show that the Kingdom of Heaven is worth any sacrifice.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Pearl of Great Price, the merchant decided the pearl was too expensive, so he walked away without buying it.
beginner.explanation: The merchant did NOT walk away. He recognised the pearl's extraordinary value and joyfully sold everything he owned to buy it. The parable teaches that discovering God's kingdom is so wonderful that giving up everything else is worth it.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Pearl of Great Price, the pearl represents the Kingdom of Heaven.
beginner.explanation: Jesus said 'The kingdom of heaven is like a merchant looking for fine pearls. When he found one of great value, he went away and sold everything he had and bought it.' The pearl symbolises the immense, incomparable value of belonging to God's kingdom.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Hidden Treasure, a man found treasure buried in a field and joyfully sold everything he had to buy that field.
beginner.explanation: When the man discovered the treasure, he hid it again and then, in his great joy, sold everything he owned to buy the field. His joy made the sacrifice feel like nothing — he knew the treasure was worth far more than everything he gave up.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Hidden Treasure, the man who found the treasure was sad and reluctant to sell everything he owned to buy the field.
beginner.explanation: The man was not sad or reluctant — he was full of joy! He recognised the treasure's incredible value and gladly sold everything. The parable shows that discovering God's kingdom should bring such joy that any sacrifice is made willingly.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Wheat and the Weeds, an enemy came at night and sowed weeds among a farmer's wheat field.
beginner.explanation: While everyone was sleeping, an enemy came and sowed weeds (tares) among the good wheat. The weeds looked similar to wheat while growing, making them hard to distinguish until harvest time. Jesus said the enemy represents the devil.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Wheat and the Weeds, the owner told his servants to immediately pull up all the weeds as soon as they were discovered.
beginner.explanation: The owner told his servants NOT to pull up the weeds, saying 'while you are pulling the weeds, you may uproot the wheat with them. Let both grow together until the harvest.' He would sort them at harvest time instead.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Wheat and the Weeds, the weeds are gathered and burned at harvest time while the wheat is stored in the barn.
beginner.explanation: At harvest time, the reapers first collect the weeds, tie them in bundles, and burn them. Then they gather the good wheat into the barn. Jesus explained that this represents the final judgment when angels will separate the wicked from the righteous.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Wheat and the Weeds, Jesus said the field represents the world, the good seed represents God's people, and the weeds represent people of the evil one.
beginner.explanation: Jesus gave a detailed explanation: 'The field is the world, the good seed stands for the people of the kingdom, the weeds are the people of the evil one, and the enemy who sows them is the devil. The harvest is the end of the age.'

beginner.question: In the parable of the Net, fishermen cast a large net into the sea and then sorted the good fish from the bad when they brought it ashore.
beginner.explanation: The fishermen cast a dragnet that caught all kinds of fish. When it was full, they dragged it to shore, sat down, and sorted the good fish into containers while throwing the bad away. This represents God's judgment at the end of the age.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Net, the fishermen kept every single fish they caught because all fish are equally valuable.
beginner.explanation: The fishermen did NOT keep every fish. They carefully sorted their catch, keeping the good fish and throwing away the bad ones. Jesus said this sorting represents the final judgment when angels will separate the wicked from the righteous.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Rich Man and Lazarus, the poor beggar Lazarus sat at the rich man's gate, covered in sores, longing for scraps from his table.
beginner.explanation: Lazarus was in a terrible state — covered in sores, with dogs licking his wounds, and so hungry he longed just for the crumbs that fell from the rich man's table. Meanwhile, the rich man lived in luxury every day, completely ignoring Lazarus's suffering.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Rich Man and Lazarus, the rich man showed kindness to Lazarus by regularly feeding him and caring for his sores.
beginner.explanation: The rich man showed NO kindness to Lazarus at all. He dressed in purple and fine linen and feasted lavishly every day while Lazarus suffered in misery at his gate. His indifference to the beggar's suffering was the central problem of the story.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Rich Man and Lazarus, after both men died, Lazarus was carried by angels to Abraham's side in comfort.
beginner.explanation: When Lazarus died, angels carried him to Abraham's side — a place of comfort, peace, and honour. Despite his terrible suffering on earth, Lazarus was now comforted in the afterlife. Their earthly positions were completely reversed.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Rich Man and Lazarus, the rich man ended up in a place of comfort after death, just like he had lived in comfort on earth.
beginner.explanation: The rich man did NOT end up in comfort. He found himself in Hades, in torment. He could see Lazarus far away at Abraham's side and cried out for help. Abraham told him the situation could not be changed — a great chasm separated them.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Rich Man and Lazarus, the rich man begged Abraham to send Lazarus to warn his five brothers so they could avoid his fate.
beginner.explanation: Realising his brothers were living the same selfish lifestyle, the rich man begged Abraham to send Lazarus back from the dead to warn them. Abraham replied that they already had Moses and the Prophets (the Scriptures) to guide them.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Barren Fig Tree, a man found no fruit on his fig tree for three years and wanted to cut it down.
beginner.explanation: The owner had come looking for fruit for three years and found none. He said to the gardener 'Cut it down! Why should it use up the soil?' Three years was more than enough time for a fig tree to produce, so his frustration was reasonable.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Barren Fig Tree, the gardener agreed with the owner that the tree should be cut down immediately.
beginner.explanation: The gardener pleaded for patience, saying 'Sir, leave it alone for one more year, and I'll dig around it and fertilise it. If it bears fruit next year, fine! If not, then cut it down.' He interceded for the tree, asking for one more chance.

beginner.question: The parable of the Barren Fig Tree teaches that God is patient and gives people time to change, but he also expects them to eventually bear fruit.
beginner.explanation: God's patience is real but purposeful. He gives people time and extra care to grow and produce good fruit in their lives. But patience has limits — it is given so we will change, not so we can remain the same forever.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Persistent Widow, a widow kept going to an unjust judge to plead for justice, and she refused to give up.
beginner.explanation: The widow went again and again to the judge, who neither feared God nor cared about people. Despite his indifference, she persisted in her plea for justice against her adversary. Her relentless determination eventually wore the judge down.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Persistent Widow, the judge was described as a fair, godly man who cared deeply about justice.
beginner.explanation: The judge was the opposite of fair and godly. Jesus specifically described him as someone who 'neither feared God nor cared what people thought.' He was unjust and indifferent. Yet even this terrible judge eventually gave in to the widow's persistence.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Persistent Widow, the unjust judge eventually granted the widow justice because she kept coming to him and wearing him out.
beginner.explanation: The judge finally said 'Even though I don't fear God or care about people, because this widow keeps bothering me, I will see that she gets justice.' He gave in not from compassion but to stop her from continually coming to him.

beginner.question: Jesus told the parable of the Persistent Widow to teach his disciples that they should always pray and never give up.
beginner.explanation: Luke 18:1 explicitly states the purpose: 'Jesus told his disciples a parable to show them that they should always pray and not give up.' If an unjust judge responds to persistence, how much more will God — who is loving — answer his people's prayers.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Two Sons, the first son said 'No' to his father but later changed his mind and went to work in the vineyard.
beginner.explanation: The first son initially refused his father's request, but afterwards he regretted it, changed his mind, and went to work. His actions, not his initial words, showed his true obedience. Jesus said this son did the father's will.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Two Sons, the second son said 'Yes, sir' to his father and then promptly went to work in the vineyard as he promised.
beginner.explanation: The second son said 'I will, sir' but he never actually went to work. His words sounded respectful and obedient, but his actions told a different story. He was all talk and no follow-through — the opposite of true obedience.

beginner.question: The parable of the Two Sons teaches that actions speak louder than words — what matters to God is not what you say but what you actually do.
beginner.explanation: Jesus used this parable to confront the religious leaders who talked about serving God but didn't truly obey, while tax collectors and sinners who repented and changed their ways were entering God's kingdom. Genuine obedience is shown through actions.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Leaven, a woman mixed a small amount of yeast into three measures of flour, and it worked through the entire batch of dough.
beginner.explanation: The woman hid yeast in a large amount of flour (about 22 litres), and the small amount of yeast permeated the entire batch, causing it all to rise. Jesus used this familiar baking image to show how God's kingdom spreads quietly but powerfully.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Leaven, the yeast had no effect on the dough — the bread stayed flat and did not rise.
beginner.explanation: The yeast had a dramatic effect, working through the entire batch of dough and making it all rise. That's the whole point — a tiny amount of yeast transforms a huge amount of flour, just as God's kingdom, starting small, transforms everything it touches.

beginner.question: The parable of the Leaven teaches that the Kingdom of God starts small but quietly spreads and transforms everything it touches.
beginner.explanation: Like yeast invisibly working through dough until the entire batch has risen, the Kingdom of God may seem small and hidden at first, but its influence spreads unstoppably. It transforms individuals, communities, and ultimately the whole world from within.

beginner.question: A parable is a short story that uses everyday situations — like farming, baking, or family life — to teach a deeper spiritual or moral lesson.
beginner.explanation: Jesus used parables to take familiar scenes from daily life and use them to reveal deep truths about God, his kingdom, and how people should live. This made profound spiritual teachings accessible and memorable for ordinary people.

beginner.question: Jesus only told parables that are recorded in the Gospel of John; the other three Gospels contain no parables.
beginner.explanation: This is completely backwards. Most of Jesus' parables are recorded in Matthew, Mark, and Luke (the Synoptic Gospels). The Gospel of John contains very few traditional parables. Luke's Gospel actually contains the most parables of any Gospel.

beginner.question: Jesus often introduced his parables by saying 'The Kingdom of Heaven is like...' before telling the story.
beginner.explanation: Many of Jesus' parables begin with phrases like 'The kingdom of heaven is like...' or 'The kingdom of God is like...' This opening signals that the story is about to reveal something important about God's rule, character, or kingdom.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Prodigal Son, the younger son planned to ask his father to treat him as a hired servant, but the father restored him fully as a son instead.
beginner.explanation: The son rehearsed a speech: 'Make me like one of your hired servants.' But the father didn't even let him finish — he interrupted with celebration, putting the best robe, a ring, and sandals on him. Full sonship, not servanthood, was restored.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Lost Sheep, the shepherd had 50 sheep and one went missing.
beginner.explanation: The shepherd had 100 sheep, not 50. When one of the hundred went astray, he left the ninety-nine in the open country and went searching for the one lost sheep. The specific number 100 emphasises that every single one out of the whole flock matters.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Sower, the seed on good soil represents people who hear God's word, understand it, and produce abundant fruit in their lives.
beginner.explanation: Jesus said the good soil represents those with 'a noble and good heart, who hear the word, retain it, and by persevering produce a crop.' These are people who don't just hear the message but truly take it to heart and live it out.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Sower, the birds that ate the seed on the path represent good angels who carry God's word to new places.
beginner.explanation: The birds do NOT represent good angels. Jesus said the birds represent the evil one (the devil) who snatches away God's word from people's hearts before it can take root. The path represents hard-hearted people who never truly receive the message.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Wise and Foolish Builders, the same storm — with rain, floods, and wind — hit both houses.
beginner.explanation: Both the wise man's house on rock and the foolish man's house on sand faced the exact same storm. The difference was not the severity of the trials but the foundation each house was built on. A strong foundation determines whether you survive life's storms.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Wise and Foolish Builders, the foolish man's house on sand survived the storm just as well as the wise man's house on rock.
beginner.explanation: The house on sand did NOT survive. It fell with a great crash — a total collapse. Only the house built on rock stood firm through the storm. The parable teaches that a life not built on obedience to God's word will not withstand life's difficulties.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Talents, a 'talent' was a unit of money worth about 20 years of wages for an ordinary worker — an enormous sum.
beginner.explanation: A talent was a massive amount of money, not a small coin. Even the servant who received just one talent was entrusted with a huge sum. This emphasises that God gives significant gifts and resources to everyone and expects faithful stewardship.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Wedding Feast, when the originally invited guests refused to come, the king simply cancelled the wedding banquet.
beginner.explanation: The king did NOT cancel the feast. Instead, he sent servants out to the streets to invite anyone they could find, both good and bad, until the wedding hall was full. This represents God's grace being extended to everyone after the original invitees rejected it.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Rich Fool, Jesus told the story after someone in the crowd asked him to settle an inheritance dispute with his brother.
beginner.explanation: A man in the crowd asked Jesus 'Teacher, tell my brother to divide the inheritance with me.' Jesus refused to play judge in family disputes and instead told the parable of the Rich Fool to warn everyone about the danger of greed.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Pharisee and the Tax Collector, tax collectors were respected members of Jewish society who were seen as honest and trustworthy.
beginner.explanation: Tax collectors were despised in Jewish society, seen as traitors who worked for the Roman occupiers and often cheated people by collecting more than required. That's what makes the parable so surprising — the despised tax collector, not the respected Pharisee, was justified.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Unforgiving Servant, Jesus told this story after Peter asked him how many times he should forgive someone who sins against him.
beginner.explanation: Peter asked Jesus 'Lord, how many times shall I forgive my brother or sister who sins against me? Up to seven times?' Jesus replied 'Not seven times, but seventy-seven times' and then told the parable of the Unforgiving Servant to illustrate.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Good Shepherd, Jesus said he is the good shepherd who abandons the sheep when a wolf comes to attack them.
beginner.explanation: Jesus said the opposite. The good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep. It's the hired hand — not the good shepherd — who abandons the sheep and runs away when the wolf comes. Jesus willingly protects and sacrifices for his flock.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Workers in the Vineyard, the last workers hired only worked for about one hour but received the same full day's wage as those who worked all day.
beginner.explanation: The workers hired at the eleventh hour worked only about one hour yet received a full denarius — the same as those who started at dawn. The landowner was generous beyond what was expected, illustrating God's abundant grace that is freely given.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Ten Virgins, the foolish bridesmaids were still able to enter the wedding feast even though they arrived late after buying more oil.
beginner.explanation: The foolish bridesmaids were NOT able to enter. When they returned after buying oil, the door was shut. They cried 'Lord, Lord, open the door!' but the bridegroom replied 'I don't know you.' They missed their chance because they weren't prepared.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Hidden Treasure, the man who found treasure in a field hid it again and then sold everything to buy the field so he could have the treasure.
beginner.explanation: The man found the treasure, hid it back in the field, and then in his joy went and sold all he had to buy the field. By owning the field, he would own the treasure within it. His sacrifice was made joyfully because he knew the treasure's value.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Wheat and the Weeds, the weeds represent people of the evil one, while the good wheat represents people of God's kingdom.
beginner.explanation: Jesus explicitly explained: 'The one who sowed the good seed is the Son of Man. The field is the world, and the good seed stands for the people of the kingdom. The weeds are the people of the evil one, and the enemy who sows them is the devil.'

beginner.question: In the parable of the Rich Man and Lazarus, Abraham agreed to send Lazarus back from the dead to warn the rich man's brothers about their selfish lifestyle.
beginner.explanation: Abraham refused the request. He said the brothers already had 'Moses and the Prophets' (the Scriptures) to guide them. When the rich man insisted they would listen to someone from the dead, Abraham said if they won't listen to Scripture, they won't be convinced even by a resurrection.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Rich Man and Lazarus, Lazarus is the only character in any of Jesus' parables who is given a personal name.
beginner.explanation: In all of Jesus' many parables, characters are typically described by role — 'a farmer,' 'a king,' 'a widow.' Lazarus is uniquely given a name, which means 'God is my help.' This naming gives the poor man dignity and personal identity in the story.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Barren Fig Tree, the gardener asked the owner for one more year to dig around the tree and fertilise it before cutting it down.
beginner.explanation: The gardener interceded for the tree, asking for extra time and promising to give it special care. He said 'If it bears fruit next year, fine! If not, then cut it down.' This represents God's patient, active work in people's lives to help them grow.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Persistent Widow, Jesus' main point was that if even an unjust judge responds to persistence, how much more will God answer the prayers of his people.
beginner.explanation: Jesus used an argument from lesser to greater: if a corrupt, uncaring judge eventually gives justice because of persistence, how much more will God — who is perfectly just and loves his children — quickly bring about justice for those who cry out to him.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Two Sons, the son who initially said 'No' but later obeyed was the one who truly did his father's will.
beginner.explanation: Actions speak louder than words. The first son's initial refusal was wrong, but his change of heart and actual obedience showed genuine repentance. The second son's polite 'Yes, sir' meant nothing because he never followed through with action.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Prodigal Son, the young man spent his inheritance wisely and became a successful businessman in a foreign country.
beginner.explanation: The son did NOT spend wisely. He squandered all his wealth on wild, reckless living. When a severe famine hit the land, he had nothing left and was reduced to feeding pigs — one of the most degrading jobs imaginable for a Jewish person.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Good Samaritan, the Levite was the second person to see the wounded man and pass by without helping.
beginner.explanation: The Levite was the second religious figure to pass by the injured man. Like the priest before him, the Levite saw the wounded traveller but crossed to the other side of the road and continued on his way without offering any help.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Mustard Seed, Jesus compared the Kingdom of Heaven to a mustard seed because it starts from tiny beginnings but grows into something enormous.
beginner.explanation: The mustard seed was the smallest seed known to Jesus' audience, yet it grew into a large tree-like bush. Jesus chose this image to encourage his followers that God's kingdom — though it might seem small and insignificant now — would grow beyond imagination.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Sower, the seed that fell among thorns produced the best crop because thorns provide protection from the sun.
beginner.explanation: The seed among thorns did NOT produce a crop. The thorns grew up and choked the plants, preventing them from bearing any fruit. Jesus said the thorns represent worries, wealth, and worldly pleasures that crowd out God's word in people's lives.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Talents, the master praised both the five-talent and two-talent servants with the same words: 'Well done, good and faithful servant.'
beginner.explanation: Both servants received identical praise despite producing different amounts. The five-talent servant earned five more; the two-talent servant earned two more. What mattered to the master was faithfulness with what each was given, not the total amount.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Rich Fool, the wealthy man's plan was to take life easy, eat, drink, and be merry for many years to come.
beginner.explanation: After deciding to build bigger barns, the rich man said to himself 'You have plenty of grain laid up for many years. Take life easy; eat, drink, and be merry.' He thought his wealth guaranteed a comfortable future — but God had other plans.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Net, Jesus said the sorting of fish represents the end of the age when angels will separate the wicked from the righteous.
beginner.explanation: Jesus explicitly said 'This is how it will be at the end of the age. The angels will come and separate the wicked from the righteous and throw them into the blazing furnace.' The parable is a clear picture of final judgment.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Good Shepherd, Jesus said that the hired hand cares about the sheep just as much as the good shepherd does.
beginner.explanation: Jesus said the hired hand does NOT care about the sheep. 'The hired hand is not the shepherd and does not own the sheep. So when he sees the wolf coming, he abandons the sheep and runs away.' Only the good shepherd truly cares.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Prodigal Son, the father divided his property between both sons — not just the younger one — when asked.
beginner.explanation: When the younger son asked for his share, the father divided his property between both of them. The older son received his portion too. However, only the younger son took his share, left home, and squandered it in a distant country.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Wedding Feast, the king destroyed the city of the guests who killed his servants when they delivered the wedding invitation.
beginner.explanation: Some of the invited guests seized the king's servants, mistreated them, and killed them. The enraged king sent his army, destroyed those murderers, and burned their city. Then he extended the invitation to anyone willing to come.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Unforgiving Servant, the fellow servant who owed 100 denarii begged for patience, just as the first servant had begged the king.
beginner.explanation: The fellow servant fell to his knees and begged 'Be patient with me, and I will pay it back' — almost identical words to what the first servant had said to the king. But unlike the king who showed mercy, the first servant refused and had him thrown in prison.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Lost Sheep, after finding the lost sheep, the shepherd scolded it and punished it for wandering away.
beginner.explanation: The shepherd did NOT scold or punish the sheep. He joyfully placed it on his shoulders and carried it home, then called friends and neighbours to celebrate. This pictures God's response to a repentant sinner: not anger, but overflowing joy.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Persistent Widow, the unjust judge eventually helped the widow not because he cared about justice, but because her persistence was wearing him out.
beginner.explanation: The judge admitted his selfish motivation: 'Because this widow keeps bothering me, I will see that she gets justice, so that she won't eventually come and attack me!' He didn't help out of compassion — he simply wanted her to stop coming.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Workers in the Vineyard, the landowner paid the early workers less than the late workers to punish them for complaining.
beginner.explanation: The landowner paid ALL workers the same — one denarius. He did not reduce the early workers' pay. He had paid them exactly what was agreed. His generosity to the late workers did not take anything away from the early ones.

beginner.question: Matthew chapter 13 contains several parables about the Kingdom of Heaven, including the Sower, the Wheat and Weeds, the Mustard Seed, the Leaven, the Hidden Treasure, the Pearl, and the Net.
beginner.explanation: Matthew 13 is sometimes called the 'Parable Chapter' because it contains this remarkable collection of Kingdom parables. Jesus taught them to crowds by the lake and then explained several privately to his disciples.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Ten Virgins, the bridegroom arrived exactly on time, and there was no delay in his coming.
beginner.explanation: The bridegroom was delayed — so much so that all ten bridesmaids fell asleep waiting for him. He didn't arrive until midnight. This delay is a key element of the parable, teaching that Jesus' return may take longer than expected and we must remain prepared.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Prodigal Son, the younger son came to his senses while feeding pigs and decided to return home to his father.
beginner.explanation: While longing to fill his stomach with pig food, the son 'came to his senses' and realised that even his father's hired servants had food to spare. He decided to go home, confess his sin, and ask to be treated as a servant rather than a son.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Good Samaritan, Jews and Samaritans were close friends and allies who got along very well in Jesus' time.
beginner.explanation: Jews and Samaritans were NOT friendly. They had deep hostility rooted in centuries of religious and ethnic conflict. That's what makes the parable so powerful — the hero is someone the audience would have despised, yet he showed more compassion than their own religious leaders.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Rich Man and Lazarus, Abraham told the rich man that a great chasm was fixed between them so that no one could cross from one side to the other.
beginner.explanation: Abraham said 'Between us and you a great chasm has been set in place, so that those who want to go from here to you cannot, nor can anyone cross over from there to us.' The separation after death was permanent and uncrossable.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Barren Fig Tree, the fig tree had been fruitless for ten years before the owner noticed.
beginner.explanation: The owner had been looking for fruit for three years, not ten. Three years was a reasonable period to expect a fig tree to bear fruit. The shorter timeframe emphasises that God is patient but does not wait forever for people to respond to his care.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Pearl of Great Price and the parable of the Hidden Treasure, both stories teach the same lesson: the Kingdom of Heaven is worth giving up everything to have.
beginner.explanation: These twin parables appear back-to-back in Matthew 13 and share the same core message. Whether you stumble upon the kingdom unexpectedly (like the man finding treasure) or search for it deliberately (like the pearl merchant), its value demands total commitment.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Wheat and the Weeds, the harvest represents the end of the age, and the reapers represent God's angels.
beginner.explanation: Jesus explicitly stated: 'The harvest is the end of the age, and the harvesters are angels.' At the final judgment, God's angels will separate the righteous from the wicked, just as reapers separate wheat from weeds at harvest time.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Good Shepherd, Jesus said 'I give my sheep eternal life, and they shall never perish; no one will snatch them out of my hand.'
beginner.explanation: This is one of the most reassuring promises in the Bible. Jesus declared that his followers are eternally secure in his care — no outside force can snatch them away. This emphasises the depth of the good shepherd's commitment to his flock.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Sower, Jesus told this story while sitting in a boat on the lake, teaching the crowds gathered on the shore.
beginner.explanation: Jesus got into a boat and sat in it out on the lake while the crowd stood on the shore listening. The natural amphitheatre of the lake shore allowed his voice to carry to a large audience as he taught them many things using parables.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Unforgiving Servant, the king initially planned to sell the servant, his wife, his children, and everything he owned to repay the debt.
beginner.explanation: Before showing mercy, the king ordered the servant to be sold along with his wife, children, and all possessions to repay the debt. It was only when the servant fell on his knees and begged for patience that the king had compassion and cancelled the entire debt.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Wise and Foolish Builders, the parable comes at the very end of the Sermon on the Mount, Jesus' most famous sermon.
beginner.explanation: Jesus used this parable to conclude the Sermon on the Mount (Matthew 5-7). After teaching about the Beatitudes, prayer, and many other topics, he ended with this vivid illustration about the critical importance of not just hearing his words but actually doing them.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Leaven, the woman mixed yeast into ten measures of flour — enough bread for a small army.
beginner.explanation: The woman mixed yeast into three measures of flour, not ten. Three measures (about 22 litres) was already a very large amount, enough to feed a crowd. The point was that a tiny bit of yeast transformed the entire large batch.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Two Sons, Jesus told this parable to confront the chief priests and elders who claimed to serve God but did not truly obey him.
beginner.explanation: Jesus was directly addressing the chief priests and elders of the people. He told them that tax collectors and prostitutes who repented were entering God's kingdom ahead of them, because the religious leaders said 'yes' to God with their mouths but didn't follow through.

beginner.question: In the parable of the Lost Coin, the woman who lost her coin had twenty silver coins and lost five of them.
beginner.explanation: The woman had ten silver coins and lost just one. She lit a lamp and swept her entire house searching carefully for that single missing coin. When she found it, she called her friends to celebrate. Each coin — each person — matters to God.

beginner.question: In the very beginning of the Bible, God created the first woman from the rib of the first man, Adam. What was this first woman's name?
beginner.explanation: Eve was the first woman ever created by God. According to Genesis, God formed her from one of Adam's ribs while he slept, and she became Adam's wife and companion in the Garden of Eden.

beginner.question: Eve, the first woman in the Bible, lived with Adam in a beautiful garden. What was this garden called?
beginner.explanation: Eve and Adam lived in the Garden of Eden, a perfect paradise created by God. It was a place of beauty and abundance where they could freely eat from almost every tree.

beginner.question: In the Garden of Eden, a serpent tempted Eve to eat fruit from a tree God had forbidden. What was this tree called?
options: The Tree of Knowledge of Good and Evil
beginner.explanation: The serpent convinced Eve to eat from the Tree of Knowledge of Good and Evil, the one tree God had told Adam and Eve not to eat from. After she ate, she gave some to Adam as well, leading to their expulsion from Eden.

beginner.question: After Eve and Adam disobeyed God by eating the forbidden fruit, what happened to them?
options: They were sent out of the Garden of Eden
beginner.explanation: God banished Adam and Eve from the Garden of Eden as a consequence of their disobedience. They were sent out into the world to work the ground, and an angel with a flaming sword was placed to guard the entrance to Eden.

beginner.question: Eve, the first woman, had two sons whose story became one of the most famous in the Bible. One killed the other out of jealousy. What were their names?
beginner.explanation: Eve's sons were Cain and Abel. Cain became jealous when God accepted Abel's offering but not his, and he killed his brother Abel, making it the first murder recorded in the Bible.

beginner.question: According to the Bible, how did God create Eve, the first woman?
options: From the dust of the ground
From a rib taken from Adam
beginner.explanation: God caused Adam to fall into a deep sleep, then took one of his ribs and fashioned Eve from it. This story illustrates the close bond between the first man and woman.

beginner.question: Sarah was the wife of one of the most important men in the Bible — the father of many nations. Who was Sarah's husband?
beginner.explanation: Sarah was married to Abraham, whom God called to leave his homeland and promised to make into a great nation. Together they journeyed to the land of Canaan, trusting in God's promises.

beginner.question: Sarah, Abraham's wife, was very old when God promised she would have a son. What did Sarah do when she first heard this promise?
beginner.explanation: Sarah laughed when she overheard the visitors (angels) tell Abraham she would have a son, because she was about 90 years old and thought it was impossible. God fulfilled the promise anyway.

beginner.question: Sarah and Abraham waited many years for the son God promised them. When the baby finally came, what did they name him?
beginner.explanation: Sarah and Abraham named their son Isaac, which means 'he laughs,' a reminder that Sarah had laughed in disbelief when told she would have a child in her old age. Isaac was born when Abraham was 100 and Sarah was 90.

beginner.question: Before Sarah had her own son Isaac, Abraham had another son with Sarah's servant. What was the name of this servant woman?
beginner.explanation: Hagar was Sarah's Egyptian servant. Because Sarah had not yet been able to have children, she gave Hagar to Abraham, and Hagar bore a son named Ishmael.

beginner.question: Hagar was Sarah's Egyptian servant who had a son with Abraham. What was Hagar's son named?
beginner.explanation: Hagar's son was named Ishmael, which means 'God hears.' An angel told Hagar to name him this because God heard her cries of distress when she fled from Sarah into the wilderness.

beginner.question: When Hagar, Sarah's servant, was sent away into the desert with her son Ishmael and they ran out of water, what did God provide for them?
beginner.explanation: When Hagar and Ishmael were dying of thirst in the desert, God opened Hagar's eyes and she saw a well of water. God promised that Ishmael would also become a great nation.

beginner.question: Sarah, the wife of Abraham, originally had a different name before God changed it. What was Sarah's original name?
beginner.explanation: Sarah was originally called Sarai. God changed her name to Sarah, meaning 'princess,' as part of His covenant with Abraham, signifying that she would be the mother of nations and kings.

beginner.question: Rebekah was the wife of Isaac (Abraham and Sarah's son). How did Abraham find a wife for Isaac?
options: Isaac found her himself at a market
Abraham sent his servant to find her
God brought her to their tent in a dream
She was a neighbor in Canaan
beginner.explanation: Abraham sent his oldest and most trusted servant back to his homeland to find a wife for Isaac. The servant prayed for a sign, and Rebekah appeared at a well and offered water to him and his camels, fulfilling the sign.

beginner.question: Rebekah, Isaac's wife, gave birth to twin boys. What were the names of Rebekah's twin sons?
beginner.explanation: Rebekah gave birth to twins: Esau, who was born first and was red and hairy, and Jacob, who came out holding Esau's heel. The two brothers would have a famous rivalry.

beginner.question: Rebekah helped one of her twin sons trick his blind father Isaac into giving him the family blessing. Which son did Rebekah help?
beginner.explanation: Rebekah helped her younger son Jacob disguise himself as Esau to receive Isaac's blessing. She covered Jacob's arms with goatskin so he would feel hairy like Esau when blind Isaac touched him.

beginner.question: When Abraham's servant arrived at a well looking for a wife for Isaac, Rebekah showed her kindness by doing what?
beginner.explanation: Rebekah offered to draw water not only for the servant but also for all his camels — a huge act of generosity since camels drink a lot. This was the exact sign the servant had prayed for.

beginner.question: Rachel was a beautiful woman whom Jacob fell in love with at first sight. Rachel was the daughter of a man named Laban. What was Rachel's relationship to Jacob?
beginner.explanation: Rachel was Jacob's cousin — her father Laban was the brother of Jacob's mother Rebekah. Jacob traveled to Laban's land and met Rachel at a well, where she was tending her father's sheep.

beginner.question: Jacob loved Rachel and agreed to work for her father Laban for a certain number of years to marry her. How many years did Jacob work?
beginner.explanation: Jacob agreed to work seven years for Laban in exchange for Rachel's hand in marriage. The Bible says the years seemed like only a few days because of his great love for her.

beginner.question: After Jacob worked seven years to marry Rachel, Laban tricked him on the wedding night. What did Laban do?
options: He gave Jacob Rachel's older sister Leah instead
He made Jacob work another day
beginner.explanation: Laban secretly substituted his older daughter Leah for Rachel on the wedding night. When Jacob discovered the trick the next morning, Laban said it was custom for the older daughter to marry first. Jacob then worked another seven years for Rachel.

beginner.question: Leah was Rachel's older sister who was also married to Jacob. The Bible says Leah had a feature often noted about her. What did the Bible say about Leah's eyes?
beginner.explanation: The Bible describes Leah as having 'weak' or 'tender' eyes, in contrast to Rachel who was described as beautiful in form and appearance. Despite feeling unloved, God blessed Leah with many children.

beginner.question: Rachel and Leah were both married to Jacob and became mothers of the twelve tribes of Israel. Which of these famous Bible figures was Rachel's son?
beginner.explanation: Joseph was Rachel's firstborn son, and he became one of the most famous figures in the Bible. Rachel had waited a long time to have children, and Joseph went on to become a great leader in Egypt.

beginner.question: Rachel, Jacob's beloved wife, died while giving birth to her second son. What was this second son named?
beginner.explanation: Rachel died during the difficult birth of her second son, Benjamin. With her last breath she named him Ben-Oni ('son of my sorrow'), but Jacob renamed him Benjamin ('son of my right hand').

beginner.question: Miriam was the brave older sister of one of the greatest leaders in the Bible. Who was Miriam's famous younger brother?
beginner.explanation: Miriam was the older sister of Moses. When baby Moses was placed in a basket on the Nile River to escape Pharaoh's order to kill Hebrew baby boys, Miriam watched over him from a distance.

beginner.question: When baby Moses was placed in a basket on the Nile River, his sister Miriam watched over him. Who found the baby?
beginner.explanation: Pharaoh's daughter found baby Moses in the basket while bathing in the river. She felt sorry for the crying baby and decided to adopt him. Miriam cleverly offered to find a Hebrew woman (their mother) to nurse him.

beginner.question: After the Israelites crossed the Red Sea and escaped from Egypt, Miriam, Moses' sister, led the women in celebration. What instrument did she play?
beginner.explanation: Miriam took a tambourine and led all the women in singing and dancing to celebrate God's deliverance from the Egyptian army. She is called a prophetess in this passage.

beginner.question: Rahab was a woman living in the city of Jericho who helped Israelite spies. What was Rahab's occupation?
beginner.explanation: Rahab was a prostitute who lived in Jericho. Despite her background, she believed in the God of Israel and courageously hid two Israelite spies, helping them escape. She and her family were saved when Jericho fell.

beginner.question: Rahab, a woman in Jericho, hid two Israelite spies on her roof. What did she hide them under?
beginner.explanation: Rahab hid the two spies under stalks of flax that she had laid out on her roof to dry. When the king's men came looking for the spies, she misdirected them, allowing the spies to escape safely.

beginner.question: Rahab helped the Israelite spies in Jericho and asked them to spare her family when they attacked the city. What sign did she hang from her window so the Israelites would know to spare her house?
beginner.explanation: The spies told Rahab to tie a scarlet (red) cord in her window so the Israelite army would know to spare her house when they conquered Jericho. She and her entire family were saved because of her faith and bravery.

beginner.question: Deborah was a remarkable woman in the Old Testament who served as both a prophetess and a leader of Israel. What was her official role?
beginner.explanation: Deborah served as a judge of Israel, making her one of the most powerful women in the Bible. She would sit under a palm tree and the Israelites came to her for disputes and guidance.

beginner.question: Deborah, the judge of Israel, told a military commander named Barak to go fight against the Canaanite army. What did Barak say to Deborah?
options: He refused to go at all
He said he would only go if Deborah went with him
He said he needed a week to prepare
beginner.explanation: Barak told Deborah he would only go into battle if she came with him. Deborah agreed but told him that the honor of victory would go to a woman, not to him, because of his reluctance to go alone.

beginner.question: Where did Deborah, the judge of Israel, sit to hear and decide the people's disputes?
beginner.explanation: Deborah held court under the 'Palm of Deborah' between Ramah and Bethel in the hill country of Ephraim. The Israelites came to her there to have their disputes settled.

beginner.question: Ruth was a woman from Moab who became famous for her loyalty to her mother-in-law, Naomi. When Naomi told Ruth to go back to her own people, what did Ruth say?
options: She said 'Where you go I will go'
beginner.explanation: Ruth's famous words of loyalty are among the most beloved in the Bible: 'Where you go I will go, and where you stay I will stay. Your people will be my people and your God my God.' She refused to leave Naomi's side.

beginner.question: Ruth was a foreigner from Moab who traveled with her mother-in-law Naomi to Bethlehem. How did Ruth provide food for herself and Naomi?
options: She gleaned leftover grain from fields
beginner.explanation: Ruth went to the fields to glean, which meant picking up leftover grain that harvesters left behind. This was a provision in Israelite law that allowed the poor and foreigners to gather food.

beginner.question: Ruth, the loyal Moabite woman, married a wealthy and kind landowner from Bethlehem. What was his name?
beginner.explanation: Ruth married Boaz, a relative of Naomi's late husband. Boaz was impressed by Ruth's loyalty to Naomi and her hard work in his fields. He acted as a 'kinsman-redeemer' and married her.

beginner.question: Ruth and Boaz had a son together. This son was the grandfather of one of Israel's most famous kings. Who was Ruth's great-grandson?
beginner.explanation: Ruth and Boaz's son was Obed, who was the father of Jesse, who was the father of King David. This makes Ruth the great-grandmother of David, Israel's most beloved king, and an ancestor of Jesus.

beginner.question: Naomi was Ruth's mother-in-law who lost her husband and both sons while living in Moab. What country did Naomi originally come from?
beginner.explanation: Naomi was originally from Bethlehem in Israel. She and her family had moved to Moab because of a famine, but after her husband and sons died, she decided to return home to Bethlehem, and Ruth came with her.

beginner.question: When Naomi returned to Bethlehem after losing her husband and sons, she told the women there to call her by a new name meaning 'bitter.' What name did she choose?
